## Copilot

Here’s the concise handoff summary and current-state readout.

## Previous thread summary

You were setting up a **DAII 3.5 GitHub technical review workflow** for repo `dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026`, targeting a **Friday, May 29, 2026** start. The agreed operating model was:

- **Jira = macro governance**
  - sprint tickets
  - ownership
  - blockers/dependencies
  - severity/sign-off
- **GitHub = micro technical execution**
  - branches
  - draft PRs
  - code review comments
  - validation evidence
  - PR approvals/merge trail

The recommended model was **Hybrid**:
- keep sprint-level parent Jira tickets:
  - DAII-25 through DAII-29
- use **GitHub PRs for every section**
- create **Jira sub-tasks only for higher-risk sections**, especially:
  - Section 7 — Innovation Scoring
  - Section 8 — AI Scoring
  - Section 10 — Anomaly Detection
  - Section 12 — Benchmarking
  - Section 13 — Performance Attribution
  - Sprint 5 End-to-End Integration Test

You also created or planned:
- a GitHub Project named **DAII 3.5 Technical Review**
- a PR template at `.github/pull_request_template.md`
- section PR naming and branch naming conventions:
  - branch: `copilot/sprint-X/section-Y-slug`
  - PR: `Sprint X / Section Y / Module Name`

That setup pattern matches GitHub’s normal PR/template workflow and draft-review process. GitHub supports repository pull request templates via `.github/pull_request_template.md`, and PR review/assignment is handled from the PR UI/sidebar. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/enterprise-cloud%40latest/communities/using-templates-to-encourage-useful-issues-and-pull-requests/creating-a-pull-request-template-for-your-repository?utm_source=openai))

## What was set up for Sprint 1

You completed initial setup for:

- **PR title:** `Sprint 1 / Section 1 / Data Integration`
- **Sprint:** 1
- **Section:** 1
- **Jira Key:** `DAII-12 / DAII-25`
- **Canonical Script:** `DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.3_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_18_2026.r`
- **Entry Point:** same file
- **Excluded Archive Scripts:**
  - `R/99_archive/*`
  - `src/archive/*`
  - `top-level DAII_3.5_*`

You also worked through creating the GitHub Project after discovering it had not yet been created, then added the PR/project metadata manually in the GitHub UI.

## Key conclusion from the code review discussion

The canonical script was judged **reviewable but not approval-ready as a clean Section 1-only artifact**.

Main concerns identified in the prior thread:

- the canonical script appears to cover much more than Sprint 1 / Section 1, including downstream logic such as scoring, benchmarking, attribution, and output generation
- the holdings-processing logic appears duplicated
- there are likely execution-order problems
- path handling is inconsistent
- the script relies heavily on global environment state

Because of that, the recommendation was:

- **do not treat the entire script as approved for Section 1**
- instead, use it as the canonical file but **narrow the PR approval scope** to the Section 1/data-integration portion only
- explicitly state downstream sections are out of scope in the PR description/comments

## Current project/process version

The latest agreed process version is:

### GitHub
Use GitHub for:
- one branch per section
- one draft PR per section
- PR template with:
  - scope
  - canonical scripts
  - entry points
  - exclusions
  - dependencies
  - review checklist
- technical comments and evidence in PR threads

### Jira
Use Jira for:
- parent sprint governance tickets
- selected high-risk sub-tasks only
- status/reporting for leadership
- blockers and sign-off

### Sprint cadence
The target review cadence discussed was:

- **May 29, 2026:** Sprint 1 introduced; Sections 1–3 PRs open
- **June 5, 2026:** Sprint 1 input due; Sprint 2 introduced
- **June 12, 2026:** Sprint 2 input due; Sprint 1 mods presented; Sprint 3 introduced
- continuing weekly through final integration review on **July 10, 2026**

## One-paragraph executive summary

You established a **hybrid Jira/GitHub review model** for DAII 3.5: Jira governs sprint-level oversight, while GitHub carries section-level execution and technical evidence. Sprint 1 Section 1 was set up around the canonical script `DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.3_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_18_2026.r`, but the script was assessed as too broad to approve wholesale for “Data Integration” because it appears to include downstream pipeline logic, duplicated holdings logic, and likely execution-order fragility. The recommended next step was to continue opening Sprint 1 Section 2 and Section 3 draft PRs and to explicitly scope Section 1 approval to only the relevant data-integration portion of the canonical script.

## Copilot

Yes — I recommend revisiting **both** the PR structure and the section breakdown.

## My recommendation in brief

1. **Keep PR #4 open**, but revise its scope language so it is clearly **Section 1 only**, not “the whole script.”  
2. **Use the 17-section file index as the master decomposition map**, but for Sprint 1 treat Sections **1–3** as separate review scopes even if they live in one file.  
3. **Create a second PR for Section 2 / Core Ingestion** and a third for **Section 3 / Source Validation**, each pointing to the **same canonical script** but with different in-scope code regions and reviewer goals.  
4. In every PR, explicitly say: **approval applies only to the scoped region, not to the full file**.

That matches the practical reality that your canonical script is a monolithic pipeline file with many downstream concerns, while your team needs smaller units for review.

## Recommendation on PR #4

PR #4 is currently:

- `Sprint 1 / Section 1 / Data Integration`
- open/draft in `dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026` ([github.com](https://github.com/orgs/duke-libraries/repositories?utm_source=openai))

I recommend **not closing it**, but **editing it** so it becomes a strict Section 1 scope-control PR.

### Why
Your attached section index shows the script contains all of these in one file:

- 1 Data Integration
- 2 Load Holdings Data
- 3 Create Master Ticker List
- 4 Load Pre-Fetched Panel Data
- 5 Create Company Snapshot
- 6 Proceed to Main Pipeline
- 7–17 downstream scoring, portfolio, anomaly, benchmark, attribution, controls, output

That means PR #4 should not imply approval of the entire canonical file. Instead, it should act as the Section 1 review wrapper for just the first section boundary you intend the team to analyze.

## Recommended changes to PR #4

### Change 1: tighten the PR body
Your current Section 2 template is generic. For PR #4, the body should explicitly say:

- canonical file is the full integrated script
- this PR only reviews the **Section 1 Data Integration** portion
- Sections 2–17 are out of scope unless separately reviewed
- archive paths remain excluded

### Change 2: add a scope clarification note
Paste something like this into PR #4:

```markdown
## Scope clarification

This PR uses the integrated canonical pipeline script as the source artifact, but approval scope is limited to:

- Section 1 — Data Integration

Out of scope for this PR:
- Section 2 — Load Holdings Data
- Section 3 — Create Master Ticker List
- Sections 4–17 of the integrated pipeline
- archive/legacy scripts:
  - R/99_archive/*
  - src/archive/*
  - top-level DAII_3.5_*

Approval of this PR does not imply approval of the full integrated script.
```

### Change 3: add a reviewer instruction
Also add:

```markdown
Reviewers should assess only the Section 1 logic and any dependencies strictly required to understand that section.
```

## Recommendation on the section breakdown itself

Your 17-part section index is useful and I recommend keeping it as the **master technical map** for the file.

But I would **not** use it blindly as the review structure without grouping rules.

## Best review decomposition for the team

### Sprint 1
Use:
- **Section 1 — Data Integration**
- **Section 2 — Load Holdings Data / Core Ingestion**
- **Section 3 — Create Master Ticker List / Source Validation boundary**

### Important adjustment
Your earlier naming was:
- Section 1 = Data Integration
- Section 2 = Core Ingestion
- Section 3 = Source Validation

But the file’s actual internal section headers say:
- Section 2 = Load Holdings Data
- Section 3 = Create Master Ticker List

So I recommend you decide between these two models:

### Option A — keep business-review names
Use PR titles like:
- Sprint 1 / Section 2 / Core Ingestion
- Sprint 1 / Section 3 / Source Validation

but explicitly map them to underlying code sections.

### Option B — align PR titles directly to the script
Use:
- Sprint 1 / Section 2 / Load Holdings Data
- Sprint 1 / Section 3 / Create Master Ticker List

## My recommendation: choose Option B
Because the team is reviewing a single monolithic script, the least confusing approach is to make PR titles match the script’s own section headers as closely as possible.

So I recommend revising Sprint 1 section PRs to:

- `Sprint 1 / Section 1 / Data Integration`
- `Sprint 1 / Section 2 / Load Holdings Data`
- `Sprint 1 / Section 3 / Create Master Ticker List`

Then, inside the PR body, add a short “business purpose” note if needed.

That reduces translation overhead and makes it easier for reviewers to anchor comments to actual code sections.

## Recommended PR strategy going forward

### PR #4
Keep as:
- `Sprint 1 / Section 1 / Data Integration`

### New PR for Section 2
Prefer:
- `Sprint 1 / Section 2 / Load Holdings Data`

### New PR for Section 3
Prefer:
- `Sprint 1 / Section 3 / Create Master Ticker List`

If leadership or Jira needs friendlier names, add them in the body:

- Section 2 supports Sprint 1 core ingestion
- Section 3 supports source normalization/validation readiness

## Suggested PR-body pattern for all three

Use this format:

```markdown
## Scope
- Sprint: 1
- Section: 2
- Jira Key: DAII-25
- Module Owner:
- Canonical Script(s): DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.3_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_18_2026.r
- Entry Point: DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.3_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_18_2026.r
- In-Scope Script Region:
  - SECTION 2 — LOAD HOLDINGS DATA
- Explicitly Out of Scope:
  - SECTION 1
  - SECTION 3
  - SECTIONS 4–17
  - R/99_archive/*
  - src/archive/*
  - top-level DAII_3.5_*
```

That “In-Scope Script Region” field is the missing piece.

## Most important structural recommendation

Add a new field to every PR template:

- **In-Scope Script Region(s):**

And optionally:
- **Out-of-Scope Script Region(s):**

This is the cleanest way to review one big file in smaller pieces.

## Recommendation for team review process

For a monolithic file like this, I recommend reviewers work in this order:

1. **Review section boundary and dependencies first**
   - what inputs does this section require?
   - what outputs does it create?
   - what prior objects does it assume?

2. **Then review logic inside the section**
   - transformations
   - joins
   - validation
   - error handling

3. **Then review hidden cross-section coupling**
   - global variables
   - reused objects
   - ordering dependencies
   - duplicated logic

That matters because your prior discussion already identified likely ordering and duplication risk.

## Specific recommendation about Section 2

Based on your file index, “Section 2 / Core Ingestion” may be too abstract if the code header is really **Load Holdings Data**.

So my recommendation is:

- If the code section is mostly holdings loading and related setup, rename the PR to **Load Holdings Data**
- If the team wants “Core Ingestion” as a governance label, put that in the PR body, not the title

Example:

**Title**
`Sprint 1 / Section 2 / Load Holdings Data`

**Body note**
`Business grouping: Sprint 1 Core Ingestion`

## Specific recommendation about Section 3

Similarly, if the file says **Create Master Ticker List**, that is likely more precise than “Source Validation.”

Unless that section truly performs validation checks, I would rename the PR to match the code.

## Practical recommendation for Jira mapping

If Jira ticket DAII-25 stays parent-level, I’d map the GitHub PRs like this:

- DAII-25 ↔ PR #4: Sprint 1 / Section 1 / Data Integration
- DAII-25 ↔ PR #X: Sprint 1 / Section 2 / Load Holdings Data
- DAII-25 ↔ PR #Y: Sprint 1 / Section 3 / Create Master Ticker List

If you need sublabels:
- `sprint-1`
- `section-1`
- `section-2`
- `section-3`

## Bottom line

### For item 1: PR #4
**Revise it, don’t replace it.**
Make it explicitly Section-1-only.

### For item 2: section breakdown
**Use the script’s actual internal section headers as the primary review units.**
That means likely renaming Section 2 and Section 3 PRs to align with:
- Load Holdings Data
- Create Master Ticker List

### Best overall approach
One canonical file, multiple scoped PRs, each with:
- same canonical file
- same entry-point file
- explicit in-scope script region
- explicit out-of-scope regions
- no implication of whole-file approval

If you want, next I can draft:
1. the **revised PR #4 body**, and
2. the **exact PR bodies for Sprint 1 / Section 2 and Section 3** using the script-aligned names.

````markdown
# DAII 3.5 Technical Review PR

## Scope
- Sprint: 1
- Section: 2
- Jira Key: DAII-25
- Module Owner:
- Canonical Script(s): DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.3_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_18_2026.r
- Entry Point: DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.3_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_18_2026.r
- In-Scope Script Region(s):
  - Section 2 — Load Holdings Data
- Explicitly Out of Scope:
  - Section 1 — Data Integration
  - Section 3 — Create Master Ticker List
  - Sections 4–17 of the integrated pipeline
  - R/99_archive/*
  - src/archive/*
  - top-level DAII_3.5_*

## Purpose
This draft PR establishes the technical review scope for Sprint 1 / Section 2 / Load Holdings Data.

Business grouping note: this section supports Sprint 1 core ingestion objectives, but approval scope for this PR is limited to the script region identified above.

## Repository-aware scope note
This PR uses the integrated canonical pipeline script as the source artifact, but approval applies only to the in-scope script region for Section 2. Approval of this PR does not imply approval of the full integrated script or downstream pipeline sections.

## Copilot usage in this PR
- [x] Explanation of existing code
- [ ] Refactor suggestion
- [ ] testthat generation
- [ ] Documentation improvement
- [ ] Error handling improvement
- [ ] Financial/statistical logic assistance

## Human validation note
> Required if Copilot-assisted financial/statistical logic appears in this PR.

Not required at initial setup stage unless financial/statistical logic is modified, generated, or materially reviewed in this PR.

## Validation plan
- [ ] Run N=25 sample for the in-scope section and required dependencies
- [ ] Compare section outputs to known-good output where available
- [ ] Review warnings/errors
- [ ] Verify expected intermediate/output schema
- [ ] Check section-specific edge cases
- [ ] Document upstream dependencies required to execute this section

## Evidence
- Output files:
- Screenshots/logs:
- Test results:
- Known limitations:
- Scope note: review is limited to Section 2 of the canonical script and excludes archive/legacy paths and downstream sections unless explicitly added later.

## Reviewer focus
- Confirm the canonical script is correct for Section 2
- Confirm the in-scope script region is appropriate
- Confirm excluded archive/legacy files are outside review scope
- Review section-level logic and required dependencies
- Identify missing tests, validation gaps, ordering assumptions, and hidden reliance on global state
- Log technical findings in PR comments; track severity/disposition in Jira

## Review checklist
- [ ] Code reviewed at section level
- [ ] In-scope and out-of-scope boundaries confirmed
- [ ] Required dependencies identified
- [ ] Tests added/updated or explicitly waived
- [ ] Documentation updated if needed
- [ ] Copilot-assisted code reviewed by human
- [ ] Jira ticket linked
- [ ] Severity-governed issues tracked in Jira
````

````markdown
  - R/99_archive/*
````

---

# Part 5 — What this will look like to reviewers

## Short answer
Yes: reviewers will generally need to **log into GitHub with their enterprise/organization accounts** if the repository is private or internal.

They are not likely to use a public anonymous link for actual review work.

GitHub pull requests, draft status, reviewers, comments, and code review approvals are account-based features. Reviewers typically access the PR directly in GitHub and leave feedback in the PR conversation and file diff review tools. GitHub’s PR review model is built around authenticated user accounts and repository permissions. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/enterprise-cloud%40latest/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/reviewing-changes-in-pull-requests/about-pull-request-reviews?utm_source=openai))

## What they will see
When they open the PR, they will see:
- PR title
- PR description
- files changed
- conversation tab
- review comments
- ability to comment line-by-line
- ability to approve or request changes if permitted

## Where they provide feedback
Yes — **the PR is the primary feedback location**.

They can provide feedback in three places:

### 1. Conversation tab
For:
- general comments
- summary comments
- reviewer instructions
- validation notes

### 2. Files changed tab
For:
- line-by-line code comments
- specific issues in a section
- suggested changes

### 3. Review submission
At the end of a review they can:
- comment
- approve
- request changes

That is the standard GitHub review flow. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/enterprise-cloud%40latest/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/reviewing-changes-in-pull-requests/about-pull-request-reviews?utm_source=openai))

---

# Part 6 — How to send work to reviewers

## Best method
Assign them as reviewers in the PR and send them the PR link.

### What to do
1. Open the PR
2. In the right sidebar find **Reviewers**
3. Add the reviewer(s)
4. Copy the PR URL
5. Send the URL by email/Teams/Slack with brief instructions

## What to tell them
Use something like:

> Please review this PR in GitHub. Review scope is limited to the in-scope script region listed in the PR body. Please leave all technical feedback in the PR comments and use the N=25 sample validation instructions to independently assess the code against expected output.

## Security/access
If the repo is internal/private:
- they must be logged into the right GitHub enterprise account
- they must already have repo access
- if they do not, the link will not work for them until permissions are granted

---

# Part 7 — How reviewers can run N=25 validation

Your desired workflow is good, but you need to make it explicit in each PR.

## Recommended model
Each PR should eventually include:

### A. Run instructions
- what script to run
- what prerequisites are needed
- which earlier sections must already be available
- what sample data path is used for N=25
- what outputs should be produced

### B. Expected output
- output file names
- output folder
- schema expectations
- key sanity checks

### C. Dependency statement
Because the script is monolithic, later sections may depend on earlier sections.

So each PR should say one of:
- this section can be run independently
- this section requires Sections 1–2
- this section requires Sections 1–3 plus setup objects
- this section can be validated only in cumulative mode

## Your cumulative review idea
This is exactly right:

- at first, reviewers run only the scoped section with required dependencies
- as more sections are reviewed, they can run:
  - Section 1 alone
  - Sections 1–2 together
  - Sections 1–3 together
- later, cumulative reviewed sections can be rerun on the N=25 sample

That gives you incremental confidence while preserving section-level review boundaries.

---

# Part 8 — What to add next for reviewer execution

I recommend adding a standard block like this to each PR later:

````markdown
## N=25 reviewer execution instructions
- Sample input set: N=25 validation sample
- Run mode: section-scoped with required upstream dependencies
- Required prerequisite sections:
  - Section 1
- Execution command/script:
  - [add exact command or script steps]
- Expected intermediate outputs:
  - [add files/tables]
- Expected validation checks:
  - [add row counts, schema checks, key values, warnings]
- Cumulative execution note:
  - This section should also be re-run together with previously reviewed Sprint 1 sections as review progresses.
````

---

# Part 9 — Step-by-step future workflow for a novice

Here is the simple pattern to follow every time.

## For each new section PR
1. Create or open the branch
2. Start a new pull request
3. Set the title using sprint/section/module
4. Paste the correct PR body
5. Create as **Draft**
6. Add labels
7. Add to project
8. Fill project fields
9. Assign reviewers
10. Send them the PR link
11. Add run instructions for N=25
12. Collect comments in the PR
13. Update code/docs/tests as needed
14. Move PR from Draft → In Review when ready
15. Resolve comments
16. Mark validation evidence in the PR
17. Approve/merge only when scope is complete

## For an existing PR
1. Open the PR
2. Click **Edit**
3. Update title/body
4. Save edits
5. Add or change labels
6. Update project fields
7. Add a clarifying comment if scope changed
8. Notify reviewers of the update

## Copilot

Yes — select **“Create draft pull request.”**

## Why
For your setup stage, a **Draft PR** is the right state because:

- the PR is being used to define **scope**
- you are still clarifying **in-scope vs out-of-scope script regions**
- reviewers should **not** interpret it as approval-ready yet
- you may still revise titles/body to match the script’s actual section structure

## Use Draft PR for:
- `Sprint 1 / Section 2 / Load Holdings Data`
- `Sprint 1 / Section 3 / Create Master Ticker List`

And keep PR #4 as draft too unless you are fully ready for active review.

## Rule of thumb
Use **Draft PR** when:
- scope is still being set
- canonical script boundaries still need clarification
- reviewers are not yet supposed to approve

Use **Ready for review** only when:
- title is final
- PR body is final
- in-scope region is explicit
- Jira link is added
- reviewers can begin formal review

So yes: **click the green “Create draft pull request” option.**

### 7. Decide your reviewer access model
Your reviewers will usually need:
- access to the GitHub repo
- a logged-in GitHub enterprise/org account
- access to the N=25 sample data and run instructions

So the practical review package is:

- PR link
- repo access
- sample data access
- execution instructions
- expected output guidance

---

## What this looks like on the reviewer’s end

They will:
1. open the PR link
2. log into GitHub
3. read the PR body
4. go to **Files changed**
5. leave line comments
6. optionally leave overall comments in **Conversation**
7. run the code locally if they have environment/data access
8. compare outputs against expected N=25 results
9. submit a review:
   - Comment
   - Approve
   - Request changes

So yes — **the PR is the main place feedback lives**.

## @sganesan_dumac

Reviewers will have their own version of the PR?

## Copilot

No — reviewers look at the **same PR**.

## How it works
A pull request is a single shared review space where:

- you edit the title and description
- reviewers see that same updated PR
- reviewers add comments on lines or in the conversation
- everyone sees the same thread and history

They do **not** get their own separate copy of the PR.

## What reviewers can do
On the same PR, reviewers can:
- read the description
- view changed files
- leave line comments
- request changes
- approve
- reply to each other

## What this means for you
If you update the PR body with better scope language, reviewers will see the updated version immediately.

So for your Section 1 PR, you should edit the existing PR so all reviewers see:

- what is in scope
- what is out of scope
- that approval is only for Section 1

## One exception
Reviewers can check out the branch locally if they want, but that is still the **same PR branch**, not a separate PR.

So the short answer is:

**No — there is one PR, shared by you and all reviewers.**

If you want, I can now give you the **exact final PR #4 body** to paste into the existing PR.

````markdown
Approval applies only to the in-scope script region for each PR, not the entire integrated script.

## Next steps
- verify PR bodies for Sections 2 and 3
- add reviewer instruction comment to each PR
- confirm Jira links
- start Sprint 1 review on May 29, 2026
````

## My recommendation
Do **not** reopen process design unless something is broken.  
You should now focus on:
- PR quality
- reviewer guidance
- first review comments
- scope discipline

If you want, I can reconstruct the missing part right now by giving you:
1. the **final recommended text for Section 2 PR**,  
2. the **final recommended text for Section 3 PR**, and  
3. a **single morning handoff note**.

## @sganesan_dumac

I want to simplify the code review for reviewers. To that end, can we include repo links to the main file, raw data, and output files. The canonical file is the main pipeline. There also a number of supporting files, both excel and R, files that are sourced by the canonical file during execution. Those files are in my local clone. Reviewers will need access to all these secondary files (utils, references, transformations, modules etc) during the review. I would like to include in each PR these files so reviewers don't have to go searching the repo. Can they run code in Copilot for the review to execution/outputs, or do they need to download the files to R etc??? Providing the piepline file is one thing but would it be better if we provide a Sprint specific R file along with the required supporting files, called during execution. wHATS THE BEST WAY TO DO THIS SO THAT reviewers are able to work swiftly through the tast?

## Copilot

Yes — the best way is to make each PR a **review packet**, not just a diff.

And no, I would **not assume reviewers can execute the R pipeline directly inside GitHub Copilot on the PR page**. GitHub’s normal PR review flow is for reading diffs, comments, and approvals; browser-based `github.dev` does **not** provide a terminal or Run/Debug, while Codespaces can provide a full dev environment if enabled. So for actual R execution and output validation, reviewers typically need either **a local R environment** or **a Codespace/devcontainer configured for R**. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/reviewing-changes-in-pull-requests/reviewing-proposed-changes-in-a-pull-request?tool=webui&utm_source=openai))

## Best recommendation

For speed and reviewer usability, do **all 4** of these:

1. **Keep the canonical pipeline file in the PR**
2. **Add direct repo links in the PR body** to all supporting files needed for that sprint section
3. **Create a sprint-specific review manifest** file listing required inputs/dependencies/outputs
4. **Provide an executable review path**
   - either a local R workflow
   - or, better, a Codespaces/devcontainer setup if your team wants browser-based execution

## Short answer to your key question

### Can reviewers run code in Copilot for review/execution/outputs?
- **Not reliably from the PR page alone.**
- Copilot can help explain code and review changes, but that is not the same as guaranteed execution of your R pipeline in-browser. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/github-copilot-chat/about-github-copilot-chat?utm_source=openai))

### Do they need to download files to R?
- **Usually yes**, unless you give them a prepared Codespaces/devcontainer environment for the repo. GitHub Codespaces supports working on PRs in a full environment; `github.dev` does not provide terminal/run/debug. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/developing-in-codespaces/web-based-editor?utm_source=openai))

## What I recommend structurally

### Option A — Best immediate approach for tomorrow
Use the existing PRs, but turn each into a **self-contained reviewer guide**.

In each PR, add sections for:

- **Canonical file link**
- **Supporting R file links**
- **Reference/input file links**
- **Expected output file links**
- **Execution instructions**
- **What reviewers do locally vs what they can do in GitHub**

This is the fastest path.

### Option B — Best medium-term approach
Add a **Sprint-specific runner file** plus a **review manifest**.

For example:
- `review/sprint-1/section-1_review_runner.R`
- `review/sprint-1/section-1_manifest.md`

This is better than asking reviewers to infer which parts of the monolithic pipeline to run.

## My recommendation on a sprint-specific R file

**Yes — this would be better.**

Because your canonical file is monolithic, a sprint-specific R file is the cleanest reviewer experience if it:

- sources the canonical/supporting code needed
- defines exactly which section is being exercised
- lists required inputs
- writes outputs to a known review directory
- avoids reviewers having to edit the main pipeline just to test one section

### Best pattern
For each section PR, provide:

- **Canonical file**: full pipeline, for traceability
- **Sprint-specific review runner**: minimal wrapper for executing the in-scope section
- **Supporting file list**: exact sourced files required
- **Expected outputs list**: files/tables/logs reviewer should inspect

That gives both governance traceability and practical usability.

## What to include in each PR

I recommend a new PR section like this:

```markdown
## Reviewer quick access

### Main pipeline
- [Canonical pipeline file](...)
- [Raw view of canonical pipeline](...)

### Sprint review runner
- [Section-specific review runner](...)

### Supporting R files
- [utils/...](...)
- [references/...](...)
- [transformations/...](...)
- [modules/...](...)

### Reference/input files
- [data/...](...)
- [config/...](...)
- [excel reference file ...](...)

### Expected outputs
- [output file 1](...)
- [output file 2](...)
- [validation log / screenshot / artifact](...)
```

## Important caveat on local-only files

You said many supporting files are **in your local clone**.

If those files are only local and **not committed to the repo or otherwise shared**, reviewers will not be able to access them from the PR.

So the rule is:

- if reviewers need it, it should be in the repo, in attached artifacts, or in a clearly shared location
- do **not** rely on your local clone as reviewer-accessible evidence

## What to do with Excel/reference/raw files

For reviewer speed, I’d separate these into 3 groups:

### 1. Required to understand logic
Put direct repo links in the PR body.

### 2. Required to execute the section
Either:
- commit them if appropriate and allowed, or
- provide an approved shared location and exact filenames/paths

### 3. Large/raw data not suitable for repo storage
Link to the authoritative storage location and specify:
- exact file name
- expected version/date
- checksum or timestamp if possible
- local placement instructions

## Best workflow for reviewers

### For lightweight review
Reviewers can stay in GitHub and:
- read the PR
- open direct file links
- inspect diffs
- comment
- use Copilot to explain code contextually

### For execution/validation
Reviewers should use:
- **local R/RStudio**, or
- **Codespaces with R configured**

That split is the most realistic. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/reviewing-changes-in-pull-requests/reviewing-proposed-changes-in-a-pull-request?tool=webui&utm_source=openai))

## Best overall design for swift review

Here is the approach I recommend most:

### In each PR, provide 5 blocks

#### 1. Scope block
- section
- in-scope script region
- out-of-scope regions

#### 2. File access block
- canonical file link
- supporting file links
- reference file links
- output file links

#### 3. Execution block
- how to run locally
- what package dependencies are needed
- what working directory to use
- what environment assumptions exist

#### 4. Validation block
- expected outputs
- known good comparisons
- review questions

#### 5. Reviewer action block
- read only
- run locally
- verify outputs
- log comments in PR

## Strong recommendation: add a manifest file to the repo

Create a per-section manifest, for example:

- `review/sprint-1/section-1-review-manifest.md`
- `review/sprint-1/section-2-review-manifest.md`
- `review/sprint-1/section-3-review-manifest.md`

Each manifest should list:

- canonical file
- in-scope region
- supporting sourced files
- required inputs
- output artifacts
- execution instructions
- known dependencies
- reviewer checklist

Then the PR body can simply link to that manifest.

That is much easier to maintain than stuffing everything into the PR body alone.

## Best answer to “what’s the best way?”

**Best immediate answer:**  
Use the PR as a review packet with direct links and execution notes.

**Best practical answer for reviewers:**  
Provide a **sprint-specific runner file** plus a **manifest of supporting files and outputs**.

**Best execution answer:**  
Assume reviewers need **local R** unless you explicitly provide **Codespaces/devcontainer support**.

## My recommended decision

For your current timeline, do this:

1. **Do not replace the canonical pipeline**
2. **Add sprint/section-specific review runner files**
3. **Add review manifest files**
4. **Put direct links to all required support files in the PR**
5. **Tell reviewers that code execution should be done in local R/RStudio unless/until a Codespace is provided**

## Suggested wording for the PR

You can add this:

```markdown
## Reviewer execution note

This PR includes direct links to the canonical pipeline, sprint-specific review runner, required supporting files, reference inputs, and expected outputs.

Reviewers may use GitHub/Copilot for code reading and discussion, but execution/validation of the R pipeline should be performed in a local R environment unless a project Codespace/devcontainer is explicitly provided.
```

If you want, I can next draft:
1. a **review-manifest template**,
2. a **review runner template for Sprint 1**, and
3. a **PR section template with placeholder repo links**.

## @sganesan_dumac

What is the bash command that pulls up the directory for our repo?

## Copilot

If you already cloned it and just want to go into it:

```bash
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

If you’re not sure where it is, find it with:

```bash
find ~ -type d -name "DAII-Framework-2026" 2>/dev/null
```

Then `cd` into the path it returns.

If you want to clone it first:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

To confirm you’re in the repo:

```bash
pwd
git remote -v
```

## @sganesan_dumac

Won't work if I have yet to clone this repo, right?

## Copilot

Right — `cd DAII-Framework-2026` only works **after** the repo exists on your machine.

If you have **not** cloned it yet, do:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

If the repo is private and HTTPS gives auth trouble, use SSH instead:

```bash
git clone git@github.com:dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

If you just want to open the folder where it would be cloned, first go somewhere like:

```bash
cd ~/Documents
```

then clone it there.

So yes:  
- **not cloned yet** → use `git clone ...`
- **already cloned** → use `cd DAII-Framework-2026`

## @sganesan_dumac

How do I remove files from the repository on Github? There are alot of old heavy data files from earlier  validation tests that should be removed. All the supporting files don't appear to be DAII...2026 so what is the best way to copy/upload those files to the repo?

## Copilot

Yes — but there are **two very different kinds of “remove”**:

1. **Remove from the current repo going forward**  
2. **Remove from Git history entirely** if old heavy files are bloating the repo

Those are not the same. If the files were already committed in the past, just deleting them now removes them from the latest branch state, but **they still remain in history** unless you rewrite history. GitHub’s docs explicitly note that deleting a file from the repo does not fully remove it from Git history; for full removal, you need history-rewrite tooling such as `git filter-repo`. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/working-with-files/managing-files/deleting-files-in-a-repository?utm_source=openai))

## Best recommendation

### For old validation outputs and heavy test data:
- **Delete them from the repo now**
- If they are materially bloating the repo, **plan a separate cleanup to remove them from history**
- Do **not** keep large transient outputs in normal Git tracking
- For future large files, use either:
  - **Git LFS** for large versioned assets, or
  - an external shared storage location for data/output artifacts not suited for Git. GitHub recommends Git LFS for large files that need to live with the repo; it stores pointers in Git and the file objects separately. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/articles/about-large-file-storage?utm_source=openai))

## Best way to handle your two goals

### Goal A: remove old heavy files
Use Git to delete them in a branch and open a PR.

If you already have a local clone:

```bash
cd DAII-Framework-2026
git checkout -b cleanup/remove-old-validation-files
git rm -r path/to/old_validation_outputs
git commit -m "cleanup: remove old validation output files from repository"
git push -u origin cleanup/remove-old-validation-files
```

Then open a PR.

If you only need to delete a single file or folder from the current repo state, you can also do it in the GitHub web UI. GitHub supports deleting files and directories directly in the repo interface if you have write access. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/working-with-files/managing-files/deleting-files-in-a-repository?utm_source=openai))

### Goal B: add the needed support files
Do **not** just upload everything flat into the repo root.

Instead, organize them into stable folders like:

```text
R/utils/
R/references/
R/transformations/
R/modules/
inst/extdata/
data-raw/
config/
review/
```

Or whatever structure best matches your pipeline.

## What I would do in your repo

### Keep in the repo
Files reviewers need to read and that are part of code execution logic:

- `.R` scripts
- small config files
- small reference files
- lightweight CSVs if necessary
- documentation/manifests
- sample/mock test data that is small

### Usually do not keep in normal Git
- large raw extracts
- repeated validation outputs
- generated workbooks
- large `.rds` snapshots
- large `.xlsx` files unless truly necessary and small
- bulky intermediate test artifacts

### If you must version large assets
Use **Git LFS**. GitHub documents this as the supported way to track larger files beyond normal Git comfort. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/articles/about-large-file-storage?utm_source=openai))

## Best way to copy/upload the support files

### If the files are currently only in your local clone
The cleanest approach is:

1. create a new branch
2. create proper folders in the repo
3. copy the needed supporting files into those folders
4. commit them in logical groups
5. push and review in a PR

Example:

```bash
cd DAII-Framework-2026
git checkout -b review/add-sprint1-support-files

mkdir -p R/utils R/references R/transformations R/modules review/sprint-1

cp /path/from/local/utils/*.R R/utils/
cp /path/from/local/references/* R/references/
cp /path/from/local/transformations/*.R R/transformations/
cp /path/from/local/modules/*.R R/modules/

git add R review
git commit -m "review: add Sprint 1 supporting files for technical review"
git push -u origin review/add-sprint1-support-files
```

## Very important caution
Before copying them in, decide whether those support files are:

- **true source-of-truth project files** → commit them
- **temporary personal/local review helpers** → maybe don’t commit them
- **sensitive or oversized data** → do not commit to normal Git

## Recommended repo structure for your review use case

Something like this would help a lot:

```text
DAII-Framework-2026/
├── R/
│   ├── utils/
│   ├── references/
│   ├── transformations/
│   └── modules/
├── review/
│   └── sprint-1/
│       ├── section-1-manifest.md
│       ├── section-2-manifest.md
│       ├── section-3-manifest.md
│       ├── section-1-runner.R
│       ├── section-2-runner.R
│       └── section-3-runner.R
├── data-raw/
├── inst/
│   └── extdata/
├── output/
│   └── .gitkeep
└── ...
```

## Best practice for output files
Do **not** commit lots of generated outputs.

Instead:
- keep an `output/` folder with a `.gitkeep` if needed
- add generated output patterns to `.gitignore`
- optionally commit only a small “known good” sample output if it’s genuinely useful for review

Example `.gitignore` additions:

```gitignore
output/*
!output/.gitkeep

*.xlsx
*.xlsm
*.rds
*.RData
*.zip
```

You may want to be more selective than that, but that’s the idea.

## If the repo is already bloated
If those old heavy files are already in history, deleting them now helps going forward, but won’t shrink the historical repo footprint. GitHub recommends `git filter-repo` to remove files from repository history when needed. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/working-with-files/managing-large-files/about-large-files-on-github?platform=windows&utm_source=openai))

That is a more serious operation because it rewrites history and affects collaborators.

### Use history rewrite only if:
- repo size is truly a problem
- old large files are no longer needed
- your team can coordinate a force-push/reset workflow

## My recommendation for you specifically

### Right now
1. **Remove old heavy validation outputs from the current branch**
2. **Add `.gitignore` rules** so they don’t come back
3. **Commit the supporting R scripts** reviewers need
4. **Commit only small necessary reference files**
5. **Do not commit bulky raw data/output files unless absolutely required**

### Soon after
6. Evaluate whether the repo needs a **history cleanup**
7. If yes, do that as a separate planned repo-maintenance task

## Best answer to “what is the best way to upload those files?”
**For source/support code:** commit them into organized repo folders.  
**For large data/output artifacts:** use Git LFS or external storage, not ordinary Git. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/articles/about-large-file-storage?utm_source=openai))

## Practical decision rule

### Commit to repo:
- R source files needed for execution
- small lookup/reference files
- manifests and review runners

### Use Git LFS:
- larger but necessary versioned binary/reference assets
- large `.xlsx`, `.rds`, model artifacts if truly needed in-repo

### Keep outside repo:
- big raw datasets
- repeated output dumps
- temporary validation artifacts

If you want, I can next give you:
1. a **safe cleanup plan** for old heavy files,
2. a **suggested `.gitignore`** for this repo, and
3. a **recommended folder layout** for the support files you want reviewers to access.

## @sganesan_dumac

If there is not clone of this repo yet then what is the best way for me to share with you the structure/directory of this repo (including all respective files)? I want to review the structure. folders and files, and make recommendations for restructuring. In addition, integrate your "Recommended repo structure for your review use case" into your final structure.

## Copilot

Best way: give me a **repo tree export**, not screenshots.

Since you **don’t have a clone yet**, the easiest ways are:

## Best options, in order

### Option 1 — GitHub web UI + recursive file list from API/CLI
If you have GitHub CLI authenticated, run:

```bash
gh repo view dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026 --json defaultBranchRef
```

Then:

```bash
gh api repos/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026/git/trees/main?recursive=1 --jq '.tree[].path'
```

If the default branch is not `main`, replace it with the actual branch. GitHub’s Trees/Contents APIs support listing repository files this way. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/v3/repos/contents/?utm_source=openai))

Paste the output here, or save it to a file:

```bash
gh api repos/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026/git/trees/main?recursive=1 --jq '.tree[].path' > repo_tree.txt
```

### Option 2 — GitHub web UI manually
In the repo, open top-level folders and copy/paste the structure you see:
- root folders/files
- `R/`
- `src/`
- `data/`
- `output/`
- `.github/`

This works, but is slower and easier to miss files.

### Option 3 — Download ZIP from GitHub, don’t clone
You can download the repo as a ZIP from GitHub, unzip it locally, then run:

```bash
find DAII-Framework-2026 -print | sort
```

or better:

```bash
find DAII-Framework-2026 \
  -not -path '*/.git/*' \
  | sed 's#^DAII-Framework-2026/*##' \
  | sort > repo_tree.txt
```

Then share `repo_tree.txt`.

## What I need from you
To review structure well, send:

1. **Full file tree**
2. If possible, also identify which folders contain:
   - active pipeline code
   - archived code
   - raw data
   - generated outputs
   - reference files
   - helper utilities
   - review artifacts

## Best format to paste here
Use something like:

```text
.github/
R/
R/utils/
R/modules/
R/references/
src/
src/archive/
data/
data/01_raw/
output/
README.md
...
```

Or attach the raw `repo_tree.txt` contents.

## Then I can do 3 things
Once you share the tree, I can give you:

1. **structure review of the current repo**
2. **restructuring recommendations**
3. a **proposed final target structure** that integrates my earlier recommended review-oriented layout, including things like:

- `R/utils/`
- `R/references/`
- `R/transformations/`
- `R/modules/`
- `review/sprint-1/`
- `inst/extdata/`
- `data-raw/`
- `output/` with generated files ignored

## My recommendation
Use **Option 1** if possible. It’s the fastest and most complete.

If you want, after you paste the tree, I’ll produce:
- **Current structure assessment**
- **Problems/risk areas**
- **Final recommended repo structure**
- **Migration plan from current → target structure**

## @sganesan_dumac

GitHub web UI manually -> we will focus on main branch for now. Is there no way on the web UI to generate the full file and branch tree in a format that I can share with you?

## Copilot

Not really from the standard GitHub web UI alone.

GitHub’s normal repo file browser is good for browsing, but it does **not** provide a one-click “export full file tree” view in the standard interface. The most reliable web-based way is to use the repo’s Git data/tree API endpoint for the branch, which returns the recursive file tree as JSON. GitHub documents this via the Git Trees REST API. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/git/trees?apiVersion=2022-11-28&utm_source=openai))

## Best web-only way
If you want to stay in the browser and not clone, use the GitHub API URL pattern for the default branch.

For your repo on `main`, the API form is:

```text
https://api.github.com/repos/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026/git/trees/main?recursive=1
```

Open that in the browser while signed in, then copy the `path` values. GitHub’s Git Trees API returns every path in that branch tree when `recursive=1` is used. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/git/trees?apiVersion=2022-11-28&utm_source=openai))

## Easier browser option
If the JSON is too messy, use GitHub CLI or API tooling. But if you truly want **web UI only**, then:

### Option A — API in browser
1. Open the API URL
2. Search for `"path":`
3. Copy all path entries into a text file
4. Paste them here

### Option B — partial manual structure
If that’s too cumbersome, give me:
- root-level folders/files
- full contents of `R/`
- full contents of `src/`
- full contents of `data/`
- full contents of `output/`
- full contents of `.github/`

That is usually enough for me to recommend a restructuring plan.

## Branch tree
For branches, the standard web UI also does not give a great exportable “branch tree” report. The branches page lists branches, but not in a nice full export format. If you want all branches in a browser-friendly machine-readable form, GitHub’s branches API is better than the UI. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/branches/branches?apiVersion=2022-11-28&utm_source=openai))

## My recommendation
Since you said **main branch only**, do this:

1. open the tree API URL for `main`
2. copy the path list
3. paste it here

Then I can:
- analyze the current structure
- identify archive vs active vs output vs support areas
- propose a final cleaned structure
- integrate the review-focused structure I suggested earlier

If you want, once you have the API output, I can also help you turn the raw JSON into a clean folder tree format.

## @sganesan_dumac

It would be alot of easier if we just cloned this repo, right?

## Copilot

Yes — **cloning the repo is by far the easiest approach**.

## Why cloning is better
Once you clone it, you can instantly:

- inspect the full folder structure
- search files locally
- list everything cleanly
- separate active code vs archive vs data vs outputs
- prepare a restructuring plan
- identify what should stay, move, ignore, or be deleted

It is much easier than trying to reconstruct the tree from the GitHub web UI.

## Best next step
Clone the repo, then generate a file tree for me.

### Clone
If using HTTPS:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

If using SSH:

```bash
git clone git@github.com:dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

## Then generate the structure
Run:

```bash
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sort
```

If you want a cleaner version:

```bash
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort
```

Save it if you want:

```bash
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort > repo_tree.txt
```

Then paste `repo_tree.txt` here.

## Even better
If the repo is huge, you can give me the most important parts first:

```bash
find R src data .github review -not -path '*/.git/*' 2>/dev/null | sed 's#^\./##' | sort
```

## After that
I can give you:

1. **current structure assessment**
2. **what should move/remove/archive**
3. **recommended final repo structure**
4. **how to integrate the review-specific structure**
5. **migration steps**

## Recommendation
Yes — clone it first. That is the fastest and cleanest path.

## Copilot

Yes — you **do not need to restructure all the new branches right now**.  
Focus on the **target structure on `dev` (or a cleanup branch off `dev`)**, then let future branches inherit that structure.

## Executive recommendation

Do this in phases:

1. **Do not refactor branch topology**
2. **Design the target repo structure now**
3. **Apply restructuring once on `dev`**
4. **Keep review PRs using links/manifests/runners**
5. **Later decide what, if anything, should be backported to `main`**

That is the lowest-risk approach.

---

# My review of the current `main` structure

## Short answer
The repo has the right **raw ingredients**, but it is currently mixing too many concerns in the root and across parallel folders.

### Main problems
1. **Too many generated outputs committed**
2. **Too many top-level pipeline variants**
3. **Active code split across both `R/` and `src/`**
4. **Archive/legacy material mixed near active execution files**
5. **Reference/raw/test/output data boundaries are inconsistent**
6. **Reviewer-facing execution assets do not yet have a clean home**
7. **Root directory is overloaded**

---

# What is already good

## 1. `R/` is actually a strong foundation
This is the best-organized part of the repo:

- `R/00_config`
- `R/01_utils`
- `R/02_ingest`
- `R/03_transform`
- `R/04_modules`
- `R/05_output`
- `R/99_archive`

That is already very close to a maintainable modular layout.

## 2. `data/` is partly meaningful
You already have:
- `data/00_reference`
- `data/01_raw`

That is a good start.

## 3. `src/archive` and `R/99_archive` are useful concepts
Archive areas exist, which is good — they just need stronger boundaries.

---

# Biggest structural issues

## A. Root directory is doing too much
The root contains all of these mixed together:

- active pipeline scripts
- historical pipeline scripts
- raw datasets
- output CSVs
- output Excel files
- plots
- config files
- `.RData`
- notebooks / Rmd / txt reports
- test artifacts
- ad hoc helper scripts

This is the biggest issue.

### Recommendation
The root should contain only:
- core project metadata
- one or two primary entry points
- major top-level directories
- lightweight docs

---

## B. Outputs are committed all over the place
Examples:
- timestamped CSVs
- timestamped configs
- xlsx reports
- pngs
- run summaries
- performance outputs
- anomaly outputs

These should not live loose in the root.

### Recommendation
Move generated outputs under a single ignored location, e.g.:

```text
artifacts/
output/
reports/
```

and stop tracking most of them.

My preference:
- `artifacts/runs/...` for generated run outputs
- `reports/` only for intentional review-ready or published reports
- `.gitignore` for transient outputs

---

## C. There are too many monolithic top-level script variants
Examples:
- `DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.0.R`
- `DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.3_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_18_2026.r`
- `DAII_3.5_PHASE_1_2_INTEGRATED_v3.5.9_FINAL_02_10_2026.R`
- `DAII_complete_execution_script.r`
- `DAII_3_5_Production_Pipeline.R`
- and many more

This creates ambiguity around:
- canonical pipeline
- approved production path
- historical reference
- review target

### Recommendation
Keep only a small number of active top-level entry points:
- one canonical pipeline entry
- one review runner entry area
- maybe one orchestration wrapper

Everything else should move to:
- `archive/`
- `docs/history/`
- or `legacy/`

---

## D. `src/` and `R/` overlap in purpose
Right now:
- `R/` looks like modularized code
- `src/` also contains modules, production pipeline files, tests, reassemble scripts, architecture code, etc.

This is confusing.

### Recommendation
Pick one rule:
- **`R/` = active executable source code**
- **`src/` = legacy transition area only**, or eliminate `src/` over time

For this repo, I recommend:
- **make `R/` the active code home**
- treat `src/` as transitional/legacy
- move only truly active code from `src/` into `R/` over time

---

## E. Data boundaries need cleanup
You currently have:
- `data/00_reference`
- `data/01_raw`
- `raw`
- root-level CSV/RDS/feather
- output-like files mixed into data areas

This should be normalized.

---

# Recommended final target structure

Below is the structure I recommend, integrating my earlier review-oriented structure.

```text
DAII-Framework-2026/
├── .github/
│   ├── pull_request_template.md
│   └── workflows/
├── README.md
├── DAII-3.5-Framework.Rproj
├── .gitignore
├── config/
│   ├── daii_config.yaml
│   ├── daii_database_config.yaml
│   ├── daii_environment.yaml
│   ├── daii_field_mapping.yaml
│   ├── daii_imputation_config.yaml
│   ├── daii_module4_bridge_config.yaml
│   ├── daii_portfolio_config.yaml
│   ├── daii_production.yaml
│   ├── daii_run_config.yaml
│   ├── daii_scoring_config.yaml
│   ├── daii_validation_config.yaml
│   ├── daii_viz_config.yaml
│   ├── test_config_n50.yaml
│   └── test_production_config.yml
├── R/
│   ├── utils/
│   ├── ingest/
│   ├── transform/
│   ├── modules/
│   ├── output/
│   ├── orchestration/
│   └── archive/
├── scripts/
│   ├── run_pipeline.R
│   ├── run_review_section_1.R
│   ├── run_review_section_2.R
│   └── run_review_section_3.R
├── review/
│   ├── sprint-1/
│   │   ├── section-1-manifest.md
│   │   ├── section-2-manifest.md
│   │   ├── section-3-manifest.md
│   │   ├── section-1-runner.R
│   │   ├── section-2-runner.R
│   │   └── section-3-runner.R
│   └── templates/
├── data/
│   ├── reference/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── processed/
│   ├── sample/
│   └── external/
├── artifacts/
│   ├── runs/
│   ├── validation/
│   └── review_outputs/
├── docs/
│   ├── continuity_packages/
│   ├── architecture/
│   ├── review/
│   └── history/
├── tests/
│   ├── testthat/
│   ├── integration/
│   └── fixtures/
├── notebooks/
│   └── dashboards/
├── legacy/
│   ├── root_pipeline_variants/
│   ├── src_archive/
│   └── experimental_ml/
└── inst/
    └── extdata/
```

---

# How your current files map into that target

## Move `R/00_config` → `config/`
These are config files, not R code modules.

Current:
- `R/00_config/*.yaml`

Target:
- `config/*.yaml`

---

## Rename active code folders under `R/`
Current:
- `R/01_utils`
- `R/02_ingest`
- `R/03_transform`
- `R/04_modules`
- `R/05_output`
- `R/99_archive`

Target:
- `R/utils`
- `R/ingest`
- `R/transform`
- `R/modules`
- `R/output`
- `R/archive`

This is cleaner and avoids numeric prefixes unless you truly need ordered browsing.

If ordering matters, you can keep them. If so, I’d still rename more consistently, e.g.:
- `R/01-config` would still be wrong for yaml though, so config should move regardless.

---

## Move active top-level runners into `scripts/`
Likely candidates:
- `run_daii_fixed.R`
- maybe `DAII_complete_execution_script.r`
- maybe the canonical v5.3 script if it remains an active runner

But I would not keep all variants active.

Target:
- `scripts/run_pipeline.R`
- `scripts/run_review_section_1.R`
- etc.

---

## Create `review/`
This is the missing piece for your reviewer workflow.

Put here:
- review manifests
- review runners
- possibly reviewer checklists
- links to outputs and dependencies

This directly supports the GitHub PR workflow you are setting up.

---

## Normalize data folders
Current mixed sources:
- `data/00_reference`
- `data/01_raw`
- `raw`
- root CSV/RDS/feather files

Target:
- `data/reference`
- `data/raw`
- `data/sample`
- `data/processed`
- `data/external`

### Suggested mapping
- `data/00_reference/*` → `data/reference/`
- `raw/*` → mostly `data/sample/` or `data/raw/`
- root datasets → classify individually

---

## Move generated outputs out of root
Current root contains many run outputs.

Target:
- `artifacts/runs/`
- `artifacts/validation/`

And ideally many should be ignored from Git.

---

## Move `experimental ML code` out of root
Target:
- `legacy/experimental_ml/`
or
- `docs/architecture/experimental_ml/` if mostly reference text

---

## Move `src/` into either `legacy/` or selectively merge
My strong recommendation:

### Do not preserve `src/` as a long-term parallel active tree.
Instead:
- keep `R/` as the main active code tree
- move `src/archive` → `legacy/src_archive/`
- assess `src/modules`, `src/consolidated`, `src/tests`, and root `src/*.R` files one by one

Some may still be active, but structurally this should be temporary.

---

# Recommended practical restructuring policy

## For now on `dev`
Yes — do restructuring there.  
No need to restructure every branch.

### Best approach
- create one dedicated cleanup branch off `dev`, something like:
  - `cleanup/repo-structure-phase-1`
- perform restructuring there
- merge into `dev`
- let future feature/review branches branch from cleaned `dev`

That avoids branch chaos.

---

# What not to do right now

## Do not:
- move everything at once without a map
- rewrite history yet
- delete all old files before classifying them
- restructure every live branch
- break the current Sprint 1 review setup

---

# What to do first

## Phase 1 — Review workflow support
Do this first because it helps reviewers immediately:

1. add `review/`
2. add section manifests
3. add section-specific runners
4. add direct file links in PRs
5. identify required support files for each section

## Phase 2 — Repo cleanup
Then:
1. create `config/`
2. move YAMLs out of `R/00_config`
3. create `artifacts/`
4. move/stop tracking generated outputs
5. define `.gitignore`

## Phase 3 — Code consolidation
Then:
1. define canonical active pipeline
2. move non-canonical top-level scripts into `legacy/`
3. decide what from `src/` remains active
4. converge on `R/` as the primary source tree

---

# Specific answer to your branch question

> As this is new, seems like we don't need to worry about restructuring this and all the new branches?

**Correct.**  
Do **not** worry about restructuring every new branch.

Instead:
- restructure once in a cleanup branch off `dev`
- merge that into `dev`
- create future branches from cleaned `dev`

Only revisit existing review branches if the restructure directly affects files they reference.

---

# My recommended final structure for your review use case

Here is the streamlined version I would actually aim for first:

```text
DAII-Framework-2026/
├── .github/
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── DAII-3.5-Framework.Rproj
├── config/
├── R/
│   ├── utils/
│   ├── ingest/
│   ├── transform/
│   ├── modules/
│   ├── output/
│   ├── orchestration/
│   └── archive/
├── scripts/
├── review/
│   └── sprint-1/
├── data/
│   ├── reference/
│   ├── raw/
│   ├── sample/
│   └── processed/
├── artifacts/
│   ├── runs/
│   └── validation/
├── docs/
│   ├── continuity_packages/
│   ├── review/
│   └── architecture/
├── tests/
├── legacy/
└── notebooks/
```

That is enough to make the repo much more navigable without overengineering it.

---

# Bottom line

## My recommendations
- **Yes**, clone now for better local inspection.
- **No**, do not restructure all branches.
- **Yes**, plan the target structure around `dev`.
- **Yes**, use `R/` as the main active code home.
- **Yes**, add `review/` and sprint manifests/runners immediately.
- **Yes**, move configs out of `R/00_config`.
- **Yes**, isolate outputs/artifacts from source code.
- **Yes**, reduce root clutter drastically over time.

If you want, next I can give you:
1. a **file-by-file migration map** from your current structure to the proposed structure,  
2. a **phase 1 cleanup checklist**, and  
3. a **starter `.gitignore`** tailored to this repo.

## @sganesan_dumac

Can you give me the cloning instructions again? Do you want to do 1, 2, and 3 above via Bash/clone?

## Copilot

Yes — clone first, then we can absolutely do **1, 2, and 3** from the local repo using Bash. That’s the easiest path.

## Clone instructions

### If using HTTPS
```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

### If using SSH
```bash
git clone git@github.com:dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

## Check that it worked
```bash
pwd
git branch -a
git status
```

## If you want to switch to `dev`
After cloning:

```bash
git checkout dev
```

If that doesn’t work, fetch first:

```bash
git fetch --all
git checkout dev
```

## Then yes — we can do the next 3 tasks from Bash

### 1. File-by-file migration map
We can inspect and classify files from the clone.

Useful commands:
```bash
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sort > repo_tree.txt
find R src data docs -not -path '*/.git/*' 2>/dev/null | sort > focused_tree.txt
```

### 2. Phase 1 cleanup checklist
We can identify:
- config files
- generated outputs
- archive candidates
- root clutter
- review-support files

Helpful commands:
```bash
find . -maxdepth 1 -type f | sort
find . -type f \( -name "*.csv" -o -name "*.rds" -o -name "*.xlsx" -o -name "*.png" \) | sort
find . -type f | grep -E '2026[0-9_].*(csv|xlsx|png|yaml)$' | sort
```

### 3. Starter `.gitignore`
Yes — we can build that from what is actually in the clone.

Useful commands:
```bash
find . -type f | sed 's#^\./##' | sort | less
find . -type f | grep -E '\.(RData|rds|feather|xlsx|png|csv)$' | sort
```

## Recommended flow
Run these in order:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
git fetch --all
git checkout dev
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort > repo_tree.txt
```

Then you can either:
- paste the relevant output here, or
- use it yourself while I guide the cleanup plan

## Short answer
- **Yes**, clone it first
- **Yes**, we can do 1, 2, and 3 via Bash against the clone
- **Yes**, `dev` is the right branch to inspect if that’s where the current review work lives

If you want, next I can give you a **copy/paste Bash block** for the full setup and inspection sequence.

## @sganesan_dumac

Aren't we looking to restructure "main" before "dev"? What is the purpose of "checkout dev" for this novice?

## Copilot

Start with **`main` for review, `dev` for change`**.

## Purpose of `git checkout dev`
It switches your local copy from the `main` branch to the `dev` branch.

Why that matters:
- **`main`** = what currently exists in the official/default branch
- **`dev`** = where you said the new review/setup files are being stored

So:
- if you want to **inspect the current published structure**, look at `main`
- if you want to **make or plan restructuring changes where active work is happening**, look at `dev`

## For a novice, the simplest rule
- **Clone first**
- **Look at `main` first**
- **Only switch to `dev` if you want to compare or prepare changes there**

## So yes — your instinct is right
If the question is:

> “What are we restructuring?”

Then the answer is:

- first, we should assess the structure of **`main`**
- then decide whether the restructuring should actually be **implemented on `dev`**

That is usually the safer workflow.

## Recommended workflow for you
### Step 1: clone
```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

### Step 2: confirm where you are
```bash
git branch
git status
```

### Step 3: inspect `main`
```bash
git checkout main
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort > repo_tree_main.txt
```

### Step 4: only if needed, compare with `dev`
```bash
git checkout dev
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort > repo_tree_dev.txt
```

## Why compare both?
Because you told me:
- `main` shows the current structure
- `dev` contains newer review files / setup work

So we may want:
- **`main` as the baseline**
- **`dev` as the likely place to apply improvements**

## Best answer to your question
No, `checkout dev` is **not** the first thing you need as a novice.

The real first step is:

1. clone repo
2. inspect `main`
3. understand current structure
4. then optionally switch to `dev` to see what has already changed there

## Simple mental model
Think of it like this:

- **main** = current official filing cabinet
- **dev** = working room where new organization may already be happening

You inspect the filing cabinet first, then organize in the working room.

## My recommendation
For you right now, use this:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
git checkout main
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort > repo_tree_main.txt
```

Then, after that:

```bash
git checkout dev
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort > repo_tree_dev.txt
```

So yes:
- **review main first**
- **use dev later if that’s where restructuring would actually happen**

If you want, I can now give you a **novice-safe exact command sequence** with short explanations after each line.

## @sganesan_dumac

"find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort > repo_tree_main.txt" - am I expecting an output from this command?

## Copilot

No — not on screen.

That command sends the output into a file named `repo_tree_main.txt` because of this part:

```bash
> repo_tree_main.txt
```

## What it does
It:
1. finds all files/folders in the repo
2. removes the leading `./`
3. sorts the list
4. writes the result to `repo_tree_main.txt`

## How to see the output
After running it, use one of these:

```bash
cat repo_tree_main.txt
```

or for easier viewing:

```bash
less repo_tree_main.txt
```

or just preview the first part:

```bash
head repo_tree_main.txt
```

and the end:

```bash
tail repo_tree_main.txt
```

## If you want output directly on screen
Run it without the `> repo_tree_main.txt` part:

```bash
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort
```

## Best practice
Keep the file version. It’s easier to save and share.

So the usual flow is:

```bash
find . -not -path '*/.git/*' | sed 's#^\./##' | sort > repo_tree_main.txt
cat repo_tree_main.txt
```

## One quick correction first
Your `find` output still included `.git`, which means the earlier exclusion pattern didn’t fully hide the top-level `.git` directory. That’s not a problem for our review, but for future exports, use:

```bash
find . \( -path './.git' -o -path './.git/*' \) -prune -o -print | sed 's#^\./##' | sort
```

Now to the real work.

# 1. File-by-file migration map

## A. Root-level files

### Keep at root
These are appropriate to remain at root:

- `README.md`
- `.gitignore`
- `DAII-3.5-Framework.Rproj`

### Move to `scripts/`
These look like runnable/orchestration entry points or one-off helpers:

- `run_daii_fixed.R` → `scripts/run_daii_fixed.R`
- `reorganize.r` → `scripts/reorganize_repo.R`
- `07_load_bloomberg_dev_cache.R` → likely `scripts/dev/07_load_bloomberg_dev_cache.R` or move into `R/ingest/` if it is true source code
- `08_ingest_bloomberg_api.r` → likely `R/ingest/08_ingest_bloomberg_api.r` if active, otherwise remove duplicate if already present in `R/02_ingest/08_ingest_bloomberg_api.r`

### Move to `legacy/root_pipeline_variants/`
These are monolithic or historical top-level pipeline variants and should not remain loose in root:

- `DAII_3.5_Complete_Pipeline_v3.1.R_2026_02_11.r`
- `DAII_3.5_FINAL_FIXED_READY.R`
- `DAII_3.5_PHASE_1_2_INTEGRATED_v3.5.9_FINAL_02_10_2026.R`
- `DAII_3.5_PHASE_1_2_INTEGRATED_v3.5.9_TEST.R`
- `DAII_3.5_PHASE_1_COMPLETE_CODEBASE_v3.5.9_02_09_2026.R`
- `DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.0.R`
- `DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.0_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_11_2026.R`
- `DAII_3.5_Test_232_Companies(20260217_194629).R`
- `DAII_3.5_Test_232_Companies.R`
- `DAII_3.5_Test_260_Companies(20260217_185851).R`
- `DAII_3_5_Hybrid_Dataset_Complete.R`

### Move to `scripts/` or `review/` after decision
This one may still be active/canonical:
- `DAII_3.5_TEST_232_COMPANIES_FINALFIXED_VERSION_5.3_WITH_DATA_INGESTION_INTEGRATION_3_18_2026.r`

My recommendation:
- keep it temporarily accessible
- eventually move/alias it as:
  - `scripts/run_pipeline_canonical.R`
  - while preserving original historical filename in `legacy/` or via comments/docs

### Move to `notebooks/` or `docs/reports/`
- `DAII_3.5_Dashboard_03312026.Rmd` → `notebooks/DAII_3.5_Dashboard_03312026.Rmd`
- `pipeline_controller_04042026.Rmd` → `notebooks/pipeline_controller_04042026.Rmd`

### Move to `docs/architecture/`
- `DAII_3.5_Master_Phase2_MachineLearning_CompleteFramework_Architecture_Code_1_23_2026.R`
- `DAII_3.5_Master_Phase2_MachineLearning_CompleteFramework_Architecture_Code_1_23_2026.txt`
- `DAII_complete_requirements_file.txt`

### Move to `docs/review/` or `docs/history/`
- `After Continuity Package 11.0.txt`
- `quartile_fix_report.txt`
- `DAII_3.5_Dashboard_04042026.txt`
- `DAII_3.5_PHASE_1_COMPLETE_CODEBASE_Modules1to9_Version_3.5.7.txt`
- `DAII_3.5_Test_232_Companies(20260217_222641).txt`
- `DAII_3.5_Test_232_Companies.txt`

### Move to `config/`
- `test_production_config.yml`
- possibly `daii_GIT_config.gitignore` as reference, or delete if obsolete

### Move to `docs/links/` or remove
- `github_raw_urls.txt`
- `github_urls_20260210_132234.txt`
- `github_urls_20260210_132708.txt`

### Remove from repo or move out of Git tracking
These look like generated outputs or bulky data:
- almost all root-level timestamped CSV/XLSX/YAML artifacts

These belong in `artifacts/`, `data/`, or outside Git.
