- `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.txt`

Those were flagged as things to keep out of reviewer-facing branch state. I have not independently verified the current repo contents or PR file lists, so I can’t honestly say “yes, cleanup is complete.” I also couldn’t reliably inspect the live repo/PR pages with the web fetcher. ([github.com](https://github.com/topics/data-repository?utm_source=openai))

## My recommendation
Before reviewers come in, do a **final repo hygiene check** focused on:

### 1. Unwanted tracked data/artifacts
Look for:
- raw data files
- processed/generated outputs
- large cache files
- local-only reviewer outputs
- `.rds`, `.csv`, `.txt` artifacts that were meant to be examples only, not committed

### 2. PR file lists
Confirm PRs #4, #5, and #6 do **not** expose:
- raw holdings data
- local checkpoint runs
- credentials/config
- bulky generated outputs
- temporary scratch files

### 3. `.gitignore` coverage
Make sure it excludes:
- `outputs/section_checkpoints/`
- local caches
- raw patent dumps
- Bloomberg cache outputs
- local run artifacts

## Fastest practical check
Run these locally from the repo root:

```bash
git status
git ls-files data
git ls-files outputs
git ls-files | grep -E '\.(rds|RDS|csv|txt)$'
```

Also review large tracked files:

```bash
git ls-files | xargs -I{} du -h "{}" | sort -h
```

And compare what’s in the active branches / PRs:

```bash
git diff --name-only dev...copilot/sprint-1/section-1-data-integration
git diff --name-only dev...copilot/sprint-1/section-2-core-ingestion
git diff --name-only dev...copilot/sprint-1/section-3-schema-validation
```

## What “clean enough for reviewers” should mean
At minimum:
- no confidential/raw source data committed
- no large generated checkpoint outputs committed unless intentionally included
- no misleading local artifacts in PRs
- only code, docs, and minimal reviewer-support files tracked
- reviewer instructions reference generated outputs, but those outputs are **produced by running the flow**, not committed accidentally

## My judgment right now
**Assume cleanup is not yet fully confirmed until you run a hygiene pass.**

If you want, I can help you with one of these next:
1. a **repo cleanup checklist** you can run line-by-line, or  
2. a **“paste me your `git status` / file lists and I’ll assess them”** workflow.

## @sganesan_dumac

Can you give me the cd command for this repo: DAII-Framework-2026


## Copilot

If you’re already in the parent directory:

```bash
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

If you want the full sequence after cloning:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

If you want, I can also give you the exact next commands to do the repo hygiene check.

## @sganesan_dumac

DAII-Framework-2026 repo clone (local) currently lives in DAII-3.5-Framework repo clone (local)

Can we untangle and make each repo clone separate and independent?

"If you want the full sequence after cloning:" -> what exactly do we need to clone here

## Copilot

Yes — you can and should make them separate.  
Right now it sounds like your local clone of **`DAII-Framework-2026`** is sitting **inside** your local clone of **`DAII-3.5-Framework`**, which is messy and can confuse navigation, tooling, and even accidental commits.

## Direct answer

You do **not** need to clone anything new if the `DAII-Framework-2026` clone is already valid.  
You mainly need to **move it out** so both local clones live as siblings, not parent/child.

---

# What “separate and independent” should look like

Instead of this:

```text
.../DAII-3.5-Framework/
    ...
    DAII-Framework-2026/
```

You want this:

```text
.../repos/
    DAII-3.5-Framework/
    DAII-Framework-2026/
```

or:

```text
.../GitHub/
    DAII-3.5-Framework/
    DAII-Framework-2026/
```

That means:
- each repo has its **own `.git` directory**
- neither repo is physically inside the other
- each can be opened independently in VS Code / terminal / RStudio

---

# What exactly needs to be cloned?

## If `DAII-Framework-2026` is already a proper Git clone
Then **nothing new needs to be cloned**.  
You only need to **move the folder**.

## If it is just a copied folder and not a real clone
Then you should freshly clone it into a clean location.

So first we need to determine which case you have.

---

# How to check

From inside `DAII-Framework-2026`, run:

```bash
git remote -v
git status
```

### If that works and shows the GitHub remote
Then it’s a real clone.  
You can just move it.

### If Git says:
```bash
fatal: not a git repository
```
Then it’s not a real standalone clone, and you should reclone it.

---

# Safest recommendation

## Option A — if it is a real clone
Move it out.

### Example
Suppose current structure is:

```text
~/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026
```

And you want both under `~/GitHub/`.

### Commands
First go to the parent of both repos:

```bash
cd ~/GitHub
```

If `DAII-Framework-2026` currently lives inside `DAII-3.5-Framework`, move it out:

```bash
mv ~/GitHub/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026 ~/GitHub/DAII-Framework-2026
```

After that, you’ll have:

```text
~/GitHub/DAII-3.5-Framework
~/GitHub/DAII-Framework-2026
```

---

## Option B — if you want the cleanest possible setup
Reclone `DAII-Framework-2026` fresh into a sibling directory.

This is the cleanest route if you’re unsure whether the nested repo is healthy.

### Example
Go to your preferred repo parent directory:

```bash
cd ~/GitHub
```

Then clone:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
```

That creates:

```text
~/GitHub/DAII-Framework-2026
```

Then you can compare it with the nested copy and remove the nested one once you’ve preserved any local uncommitted work.

---

# Important caution before moving or recloning

Before doing anything destructive, check whether the nested repo has uncommitted changes.

From inside the nested `DAII-Framework-2026`:

```bash
git status
```

If there are local changes, do **not** delete or overwrite anything until you preserve them.

You can preserve them by:
- committing them,
- stashing them,
- or copying changed files elsewhere temporarily.

---

# Best practical path for you

Because you may have work in progress, I’d suggest this order:

## Step 1 — check whether the nested repo is real
Inside `DAII-Framework-2026`:

```bash
git status
git remote -v
```

## Step 2 — if it’s a real clone, move it out
Example:

```bash
mv /path/to/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026 /path/to/desired-parent/DAII-Framework-2026
```

## Step 3 — verify after move
Then:

```bash
cd /path/to/desired-parent/DAII-Framework-2026
git status
git remote -v
```

If that works, you’re good.

---

# Answer to your specific question

## “What exactly do we need to clone here?”
Only this, **if** you decide to reclone fresh or if the current nested folder is not a valid clone:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
```

You do **not** need to clone `DAII-3.5-Framework` again if you already have it.

---

# My recommendation
**First verify whether the nested `DAII-Framework-2026` is already a real clone.**  
If yes, just move it out and keep both as sibling folders.

If you want, I can help you do this **step-by-step with exact commands** once you tell me:
1. your current terminal path, and  
2. the output of:
```bash
pwd
ls
```

## @sganesan_dumac

Provide Bash "cd" script to pull up DAII 2026 repo?

## Copilot

If `DAII-Framework-2026` is inside your current local `DAII-3.5-Framework` clone, the direct Bash command is:

```bash
cd DAII-Framework-2026
```

If you are somewhere else and want a safer version that finds it relative to `DAII-3.5-Framework`, use:

```bash
cd /path/to/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026
```

If you want a small reusable Bash snippet:

```bash
REPO_ROOT="/path/to/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026"
cd "$REPO_ROOT"
pwd
```

If you are already in `DAII-3.5-Framework`, then the simplest “pull up DAII 2026 repo” script is just:

```bash
cd DAII-Framework-2026
pwd
```

If you want, I can also give you:
1. a script to **move it out** so both repos are independent, or  
2. a script to **verify whether this nested folder is a real Git clone**.

## Copilot

Yes — this is exactly the kind of cleanup you should do **before reviewers come in**.

And the `cd` failed because you are currently in your home directory `~`, not in the parent directory that contains the repo. The error means there is no folder named `DAII-Framework-2026` directly under `~`.

# Immediate answer

## 1. Why `cd DAII-Framework-2026` failed
You were here:

```bash
sganesan@DESKTOP-KNAKJFM MINGW64 ~
```

So Bash looked for:

```bash
~/DAII-Framework-2026
```

and did not find it.

## 2. Yes, those files should generally **not** be in `DAII-Framework-2026`
From the list, these look like:
- generated outputs
- reports
- score exports
- benchmark exports
- run snapshots
- config snapshots
- workspace files

Those are almost certainly **review-noise** at best, and in some cases should never be committed there.

---

# What I recommend right now

We should do this in order:

## Step 1 — find where the repo actually is
Run:

```bash
find ~ -maxdepth 4 -type d -name "DAII-Framework-2026"
```

In Git Bash on Windows, this should work.  
If it returns a path, that tells us exactly where to `cd`.

If `find` is slow, try:

```bash
cd ~
ls
```

and then inspect likely folders like:
- `Documents`
- `Desktop`
- `GitHub`
- `repos`

For example:

```bash
cd ~/Documents
find . -maxdepth 4 -type d -name "DAII-Framework-2026"
```

---

## Step 2 — once found, enter it
Example, if it returns something like:

```bash
/c/Users/sganesan/Documents/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026
```

then do:

```bash
cd /c/Users/sganesan/Documents/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026
pwd
git status
```

---

## Step 3 — check whether those files are tracked
Once inside the repo, run:

```bash
git ls-files | grep -E '2026|DAII_3\.5_Run_'
```

That will tell you which of those files are actually tracked by Git.

Also run:

```bash
git status --short
```

to see untracked/modified files.

---

# My judgment on the file list you pasted

These strongly look like files that do **not belong in `DAII-Framework-2026`**:

## Generated timestamped exports
Examples:
- `20260331_115314_01_company_features_full.csv`
- `20260408_170754_25_ai_radar_data.csv`
- `20260326_105759_00_complete_report.xlsx`

These look like:
- pipeline outputs
- reports
- analysis artifacts
- benchmark exports

These should usually live in:
- ignored output directories
- a separate results/archive repo
- local storage
- possibly `DAII-3.5-Framework` if that repo is your broader historical workspace and is intentionally used for archives

## Workspace/state files
Examples:
- `DAII_3.5_Run_20260209_202951_Phase1_Workspace.RData`
- `DAII_3.5_Run_20260210_123521_12_module4_workspace.RData`

These especially should **not** be in a clean reviewer-facing repo.

## Applied config snapshots / execution summaries
Examples:
- `..._16_config.yaml`
- `..._11_applied_config.yaml`
- `..._07_execution_summary.yaml`

These may be useful locally, but they are usually run artifacts, not core source-controlled repo contents.

---

# Likely cleanup categories

## Probably remove from `DAII-Framework-2026`
- timestamped `.csv` outputs
- `.xlsx` reports
- `.RData` workspace files
- generated `.yaml` run configs
- console output text dumps
- feature importance exports
- anomaly outputs
- portfolio outputs
- benchmark outputs

## Probably keep only if intentionally source/reference data
Maybe keep if truly intentional and documented:
- stable reference data
- hand-curated sample files
- canonical minimal reviewer sample files
- configuration templates, not run outputs

---

# Safe cleanup workflow

Do **not** start deleting blindly yet.  
First locate the repo and inspect tracked status.

Then use this workflow.

## A. Inside the repo
```bash
pwd
git status --short
```

## B. List likely junk that is tracked
```bash
git ls-files | grep -E '\.(csv|xlsx|RData|yaml|txt)$'
```

## C. If files are tracked and should be removed from repo but kept locally
Use:

```bash
git rm --cached filename
```

This removes from Git tracking but keeps the local file if done carefully only with `--cached`.

Example:
```bash
git rm --cached 20260331_115314_01_company_features_full.csv
```

If many files match a pattern, we can batch them.

## D. Add ignore rules
You likely need `.gitignore` entries for patterns like:

```gitignore
# generated outputs
*.RData
*_complete_report.xlsx
*_company_features_full.csv
*_ai_scores.csv
*_discovery_*.csv
*_anomaly_scores_full.csv
*_feature_importance_default.csv
*_performance.csv
*_config.yaml
*_portfolio_*.csv
*_benchmark_*.csv
*_ai_radar_data.csv
*_unicorn_watchlist.csv
*_consoleoutput.txt

# run artifacts
DAII_3.5_Run_*
outputs/section_checkpoints/
```

But this should be tuned carefully so you don’t accidentally ignore files that matter.

## Copilot

Yes — **look at this as a stop-and-clean signal**.

Direct answer: **the repo is not cleaned up enough yet for reviewers.**

You have two separate problems:

1. **untracked local raw/processed data and notes** sitting in the repo working tree, and  
2. **a large amount of tracked historical/generated artifacts already committed** in the repo.

That means if reviewers come in now, they may see:
- confusing file clutter
- unclear source-of-truth code
- output artifacts mixed with source
- potential accidental commits of local raw data

## What stands out immediately

### Untracked files that should not be sitting loose in this repo
From `git status --short`:

- `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.txt`
- `data/01_raw/current_holdings.rds`
- `data/01_raw/daily_ratios.rds`
- `data/01_raw/european_patents.rds`
- `data/01_raw/patent_data.rds`
- `data/01_raw/uspto_bulk/`
- `data/01_raw/wipo_checklist.md`
- `data/01_raw/wipo_quick_reference.txt`
- `data/02_processed/`

These are exactly the kinds of files I’d want **out of the reviewer-facing working tree**, unless a small subset is intentionally tracked and documented.

### Already tracked clutter
Your `git ls-files` output confirms the repo contains:
- many timestamped output CSVs/XLSX/YAMLs
- run artifacts
- workspace files
- historical pipeline scripts
- experimental ML code
- output folders under `data/output` and `data/03_output`

That is a lot of historical/process noise for a repo you want reviewers to navigate cleanly.

---

# My judgment

## For immediate Sprint 1 review readiness:
You do **not** necessarily need to refactor the whole repository right now.

But you **do** need to do a **practical reviewer hygiene cleanup** before inviting reviewers in.

That means:

### Must do now
- remove/relocate untracked raw and processed local files from the working tree
- ensure they won’t accidentally get committed
- confirm PR branches do not include this junk
- possibly add/update `.gitignore`

### Strongly recommended soon
- reduce tracked historical artifacts in the repo over time
- but that can be a separate cleanup effort if it would be too disruptive right now

---

# What I would do right now

## Phase 1 — protect the review immediately
Move the untracked local files out of the repo working tree.

Since you said these can live in `DAII-3.5-Framework`, that’s fine.

### Create a holding area outside this repo
From where you are now:

```bash
mkdir -p /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding
```

### Move the obvious untracked files/directories out
```bash
mv DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.txt /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
mv data/01_raw/current_holdings.rds /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
mv data/01_raw/daily_ratios.rds /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
mv data/01_raw/european_patents.rds /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
mv data/01_raw/patent_data.rds /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
mv data/01_raw/uspto_bulk /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
mv data/01_raw/wipo_checklist.md /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
mv data/01_raw/wipo_quick_reference.txt /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
mv data/02_processed /c/Users/sganesan/DAII-3.5-Framework/_daii2026_local_holding/
```

Then check:

```bash
git status --short
```

If that comes back clean, that’s a major improvement.

---

## Phase 2 — prevent recurrence
Add `.gitignore` rules.

First inspect current `.gitignore`:

```bash
cat .gitignore
```

You likely need entries roughly like:

```gitignore
# local raw / processed data
data/01_raw/*.rds
data/01_raw/uspto_bulk/
data/01_raw/wipo_*
data/02_processed/

# local review/output artifacts
outputs/section_checkpoints/

# local notes / scratch
*.RData
*_consoleoutput.txt
```

But I’d want to see the current `.gitignore` before telling you the exact final block.

---

## Phase 3 — assess tracked clutter strategically
The tracked files are a bigger issue, but I would separate that into:

### A. review blocker
Are any of these tracked files actually showing up in PRs #4/#5/#6 or confusing the reviewer path?

### B. repo cleanup initiative
Which tracked historical artifacts should eventually be removed from version control, archived elsewhere, or moved under clearly labeled archive/output folders?

You probably do **not** want to do a giant tracked-history purge right this second unless you’re prepared for a large cleanup commit.

---

# What I think is happening structurally

This repo looks like it has become a mix of:
- active Sprint 1 review code
- legacy/historical code snapshots
- generated outputs
- experiment outputs
- dashboard artifacts
- reports
- ML experiments
- reviewer work products

That’s workable internally, but not ideal for clean external-style code review.

For Sprint 1, the fastest win is:
- make the **working tree clean**
- keep PR instructions tightly scoped
- avoid surfacing local raw/output noise to reviewers

## Copilot

Those are the **tracked clutter problem**.

Direct answer: **yes, most of that long CSV/XLSX/YAML/RData list does not belong in a clean reviewer-facing source repo** unless you have a very explicit reason to keep specific files. They look like **generated outputs and run artifacts**, not source code or stable reference assets.

## How I’d classify that long list

### Almost certainly should not stay tracked in `DAII-Framework-2026`
These look like generated run outputs:

- `2026..._01_company_features_full.csv`
- `2026..._02_ai_scores.csv`

### Also probably should not stay tracked

- `DAII_3.5_Phase2_Run_...`

- run summary/config snapshots

Again, these are run artifacts.

Some files in the long list are not just outputs; they may be:
- old script snapshots
- archived historical code
- experimental code
- continuity docs

Examples:
- `DAII_3.5_PHASE_1_COMPLETE_CODEBASE_v3.5.9_02_09_2026.R`
- `DAII_3.5_Master_...`
- `docs/continuity_packages/...`
- `R/99_archive/...`
- `experimental ML code/...`

These may still be clutter, but they are not the same category as generated outputs. I would treat them separately.

---

# Important distinction

## There are two different cleanup questions

### 1. Should these files be in the repo at all?
For most of the timestamped outputs: **probably no**.

### 2. Do you need to remove them before reviewer access?
**Not necessarily all at once**, but you should at least decide whether they create review confusion.

Because the big risk is not only “repo purity”; it’s:
- reviewers getting overwhelmed
- reviewers confusing outputs with source-of-truth artifacts
- branch diffs being noisy
- future accidental edits/commits to stale outputs

---

# My recommendation on those long-list files

## Short version
### Yes, most of them should eventually be removed from Git tracking in `DAII-Framework-2026`.

But:

## Do not start mass-deleting tracked files blindly today
Because that could create:
- a huge cleanup diff
- branch conflicts
- risk of losing artifacts you may still want archived

Instead, do this in a controlled way.

---

# Best practical plan

## Phase A — immediate reviewer readiness
Do **not** let those files block Sprint 1 review if they are already tracked and not changing in PRs #4–#6.

Instead:
- clean the working tree first
- make PR instructions tightly scoped
- ensure reviewers know exactly what files matter

That gets you “good enough” faster.

## Phase B — tracked artifact cleanup PR
Create a separate cleanup effort for tracked outputs.

That cleanup would:
- remove generated outputs from version control
- move anything worth keeping into archive storage or another repo/folder
- update `.gitignore`

This is much safer than mixing huge repo hygiene changes into Sprint 1 review PRs.

---

# What I think should happen to the long-list output files

## Probably remove from Git tracking
All or most of:
- timestamped output CSVs
- report XLSX files
- run config snapshots
- performance exports
- benchmark exports
- portfolio exports
- anomaly outputs
- feature importance outputs
- `.RData` workspaces

## Probably keep elsewhere if needed
If you want them for recordkeeping:
- move to `DAII-3.5-Framework`
- move to a local archive folder
- move to a shared archive location
- maybe keep a very small sample set if intentionally documented

---

# Criteria for deciding whether a tracked file should stay

Ask this for each class of file:

## Keep if:
- it is source code
- it is a stable reference input required for reproducible review
- it is documentation
- it is a minimal sample intentionally included for reviewers

## Remove from tracking if:
- it is generated by a run
- it is timestamped output
- it is a report artifact
- it is a workspace dump
- it is a local execution snapshot
- it can be regenerated from source

Most of your long list falls into the second bucket.

---

# My opinion on urgency

## Untracked raw/processed files
**Urgent now** — clean before reviewers.

## Tracked timestamped outputs
**Important, but can be handled in a dedicated cleanup step** if they are not actively in the PR diffs.

That said, if you want the repo to feel professional and reviewable, this cleanup should happen relatively soon.

---

# If you want a concrete next step
I can help you generate one of these:

## Option 1 — a **tracked artifact cleanup shortlist**
I categorize your long list into:
- remove now
- archive but keep elsewhere
- review later

## Option 2 — a **safe Git cleanup plan**
Including:
- how to remove tracked generated outputs with `git rm --cached`
- how to preserve them locally
- how to add ignore rules
- how to make this a separate cleanup commit/PR

## Option 3 — a **pattern-based cleanup command set**
Example: remove all tracked files matching timestamped output patterns, without naming one-by-one.

My recommendation is **Option 1 first**, then Option 2.

## @sganesan_dumac

Back to PR 4: 

All 3 reviewers (not including me) can access the repo (I checked). Once they're in the repo then they will proceed to clone the repo to their hard drives. Since the clone is theirs, is there any need for them to do anything with the files in the repo. Can't they simple source(" ") the pipeline file in R Studio and let the pipeline script manage the rest of required data/script files? For the review, we have DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r and run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R. Which of these two do they start or is the 2nd called from the 1st as I recall, and am just chekcign with you?

## Copilot

Yes — **once they clone the repo, their local clone is their own local copy**, so they do **not** need to “do anything” to the files in the sense of reorganizing or fixing the repo structure just to review it. Cloning creates a full local copy of the repository at that point in time, and any local changes they make stay local unless they choose to commit and push them. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/creating-and-managing-repositories/cloning-a-repository?tool=desktop&utm_source=openai))

For **PR #4 review**, they should **start with**:

- **`run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R`** first
- then **`DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`**

They should **not** start by sourcing the big pipeline file directly unless you intentionally want them bypassing the reviewer wrapper. Based on your review design, the wrapper is the reviewer entrypoint because it is the piece expected to:
- turn on review mode,
- apply the N=25 sample,
- manage section-stop/checkpoint behavior,
- and call into the canonical pipeline.  

So the mental model is:

- **`run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R` = reviewer launcher / orchestration entrypoint**
- **`DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` = canonical pipeline under review**

## Direct answer to your two-file question
### They should start with:
**`run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R`**

### And yes, the expected flow is:
**wrapper script → canonical pipeline script**

In other words, the second is not the one they manually start first; it is the one the wrapper should invoke/source as part of the review flow.

## So what should reviewers actually do?
For PR #4, the clean reviewer path is:

1. clone repo
2. open repo in RStudio
3. read PR #4 body + quick-start comment
4. open `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R`
5. confirm how review mode / sample / stop logic are configured
6. run the wrapper
7. inspect Section 1 outputs
8. then inspect `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` and supporting files as needed

## Should they just `source("DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r")`?
**Probably not as the default reviewer instruction.**

Why:
- that may skip reviewer-specific setup
- it may not set `RUN_REVIEW_MODE`
- it may not supply the N=25 sample path correctly
- it may not stop at the intended section boundary
- it may create ambiguity about what outputs to inspect

So unless the wrapper is broken, your reviewer instructions should say:

```r
source("run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R")
```

or, from shell:

```bash
Rscript run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R
```

depending on whether you want RStudio or terminal execution.

## Best wording for reviewers
I’d tell them:

> Please use `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R` as the reviewer entrypoint.  
> It is intended to configure review mode, apply the N=25 sample, and generate reviewer checkpoints for Sections 1–3.  
> For PR #4, review only the Section 1 code path and Section 1 outputs.

## One caveat
This assumes the wrapper actually does what we think it does. If you want total certainty, the fastest validation is for **you** to open `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R` and confirm it explicitly:
- sets review mode
- references the sample
- calls/sources `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`

If you want, I can help you draft the **exact reviewer run instruction text** for PR #4 now — short and unambiguous.

## Copilot

Here’s the **exact reviewer run instruction text** I’d use for **PR #4**.

---

## Short version for PR #4 comment

```markdown
**Reviewer run instruction**

Please use `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R` as the reviewer entrypoint for this PR.

Recommended flow:
1. Clone the repository locally.
2. Open the repo in RStudio.
3. Review the PR body and reviewer quick-start instructions.
4. Open `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R` first.
5. Run the wrapper script from a clean R session.
6. Use the generated Section 1 checkpoint outputs for review.

**Do not start by directly sourcing** `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` **unless needed for code inspection only.**  
The wrapper is intended to configure review mode, apply the N=25 reviewer sample, and manage checkpoint/section-stop behavior.

For this PR, please review only:
- the **Section 1** code path, and
- the generated **Section 1** reviewer artifacts.
```

---

## Slightly more explicit version

```markdown
**Reviewer run instruction**

For PR #4, please use `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R` as the primary reviewer launcher.

**Expected reviewer flow**
1. Clone the repository locally.
2. Open the repository in RStudio.
3. Read the PR body and follow-up reviewer guidance.
4. Open `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R` first to understand the reviewer path.
5. Run the wrapper from a clean R session:
   ```r
   source("run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R")
   ```
6. Inspect the generated reviewer outputs under:
   `outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/section1/`

**Code inspection flow**
- Use `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` as the canonical pipeline file under review.
- Open supporting Section 1 files listed in the PR body as needed.

**Important**
Please do **not** use `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` as the default execution entrypoint for reviewer validation.  
The wrapper script is intended to:
- enable review mode,
- apply the N=25 reviewer sample,
- and manage checkpoint / section-stop behavior.

For this PR, approval applies only to the **Section 1** review scope.
```

---

## My recommendation
Use the **second version** in PR #4, because it answers the exact confusion point:
- which file do I run first?
- what is the role of each script?
- where do I inspect outputs?

---

## One more practical note
Yes, since the clone is theirs, they generally should not need to rearrange repo files.  
But they **may still need local prerequisites** such as:
- working R environment
- required packages
- DB access if applicable
- any data/cache dependencies your wrapper expects

So “their own clone” does **not** automatically mean “zero setup,” only that they are not modifying your repo structure.

2. a **very short one-paragraph explanation of the difference between the two scripts** for non-technical reviewers.

## @sganesan_dumac

Verify that all the files required by reviewers are in dev branch? If some are in dev and some have yet to transfered (still in main) then will this present a problem?

## Copilot

Yes — **this can absolutely present a problem** if reviewers clone the repo and land on one branch while required reviewer files only exist on another.

When someone clones a repository, Git checks out the **default branch** locally first, not an arbitrary branch. GitHub’s docs state that the default branch is the initial branch checked out on clone. ([docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/proposing-changes-to-your-work-with-pull-requests/about-branches?utm_source=openai))

## Direct answer

### If all required reviewer files are on `dev`
Then reviewers should be fine **if**:
- they either clone and the default branch is already `dev`, or
- they explicitly switch to `dev` after cloning.

### If some required files are only on `main` and others only on `dev`
Then **yes, that is a problem** unless reviewers are told exactly:
- which branch to use, and
- whether they must switch branches to find needed files.

For your review flow, you want reviewers working from **one clear branch state**, ideally **all required files on `dev`**.

## Best practice for your case
Before reviewers start:
1. confirm **all required review materials** are present on `dev`
2. make sure reviewers are told to use `dev`
3. ideally make `dev` the operational review branch for this sprint, or explicitly tell them to check it out after cloning

## What files should be present on `dev` for PR #4 review
At minimum, I’d verify these are on `dev`:

- `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`
- `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R`
- `R/01_utils/review_support.R`
- `R/02_ingest/01_company_resolution.r`
- `R/02_ingest/02_pull_rd_history.r`
- `R/03_transform/01_create_snapshot.r`
- `data/00_reference/n25_representative_sample.csv`
- any other helper/config files the wrapper assumes

## The actual risk if files are split across branches
A reviewer might:
- clone the repo
- open the default branch
- not see the wrapper or the canonical review script
- think the instructions are wrong
- or run the wrong version of the code

That’s exactly the kind of friction you want to avoid before active review.

## What you should tell reviewers
If `dev` is the right branch, tell them explicitly:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
git checkout dev
```

Then in RStudio, open and run from there.

## My recommendation
**Do not rely on reviewers to discover the right branch on their own.**  
For Sprint 1 review, you want:
- one branch,
- one reviewer entrypoint,
- one expected file layout.

## What I can help with next
I can help you do either of these:

1. give you a **branch verification checklist** you can run locally right now, or  
2. give you the exact **Git commands to verify whether those reviewer files exist on `dev` vs `main`**.

Best next move is #2. Here are the commands.

### Check current branch
```bash
git branch --show-current
```

## Copilot

Got it — I can follow from this.

**What this chunk shows:**
- You successfully found the repo locally at:
  `~/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026`
- You had uncommitted changes on `cleanup/repo-structure-phase-1`, so switching to `dev` initially failed.
- You fixed that correctly with:
  `git stash push -u ...`
- You switched to `dev` and pulled updates successfully.
- The first pull brought in:
  - `R/01_utils/review_support.R`
  - `run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R`
  - `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`
- At first, the grep returned nothing on `dev`, then after a later `git pull origin dev` it showed the expected review hooks. That means the needed changes landed on `dev` in the later commit `f1f9d69`.
- Your current blocker is **not Git** anymore. It is:
  `bash: Rscript: command not found`

**That means:**
- The review runner script exists.
- The pipeline file contains the expected review-mode code.
- But your shell cannot find the R command-line executable, so the script never ran.
- Because it never ran, `outputs/section_checkpoints/...` does not exist yet.

**Also, this command was invalid:**
```bash
ls outputs/section_checkpoints/<run_folder>/section1
```
`<run_folder>` was placeholder text, not something to type literally.

**Most likely next step**
You need to verify/install R and ensure `Rscript` is on your PATH.

Try these in Git Bash:
```bash
which Rscript
which R
echo $PATH
```

If those return nothing, check whether R is installed in Windows:
```bash
ls "/c/Program Files/R"
ls "/c/Program Files/R/"*/bin
ls "/c/Program Files/R/"*/bin/Rscript.exe
```

If that last command finds `Rscript.exe`, run it directly, e.g.:
```bash
"/c/Program Files/R/R-4.4.1/bin/Rscript.exe" run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R
```
(adjust version folder as needed)

If it works, then list generated outputs with:
```bash
find outputs/section_checkpoints -maxdepth 3 -type f
```

And then inspect the newest run folder:
```bash
ls outputs/section_checkpoints
```

Then:
```bash
ls outputs/section_checkpoints/<actual_run_folder>/section1
ls outputs/section_checkpoints/<actual_run_folder>/section2
ls outputs/section_checkpoints/<actual_run_folder>/section3
```

**One more note**
This line in your pasted terminal:
```bash
$ sganesan@DESKTOP-KNAKJFM MINGW64 ~/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026 (dev)
```
was just your prompt accidentally pasted back into the shell, which caused the later syntax errors. Nothing deeper is wrong there.

Send the next chunk.

This chunk shows the key state change: **the review run did happen at some point, but your current working tree is now dirty on `dev`, and the expected section artifacts are not present under those run folders.**

### What we can conclude
- `R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R` exists on both `main` and `dev`, and there’s **no branch diff** for that file:
  ```bash
  git diff --name-status main..dev -- R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R
  ```
  returned nothing.

- But your **working tree** on `dev` has local modifications:
  - `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`
  - `R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R`
  - untracked `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.txt`

- `outputs/section_checkpoints` exists and contains:
  - `run_20260601_205754/`
  - `run_20260602_112303/`
  - `run_20260602_181952/`

So some process created run directories earlier.

### Important interpretation
This:
```bash
ls outputs/section_checkpoints/run_*/section1
```
failing does **not** mean the run folders are fake. It means the shell pattern did not match any existing `section1` subdirectories. So likely one of these is true:
1. the run folders are empty,
2. they contain differently named subfolders/files,
3. the review script created only top-level run folders before failing,
4. the structure is not `run_xxx/section1`.

### Best next commands
You should inspect the actual directory tree, not assume the subpaths.

Run:
```bash
find outputs/section_checkpoints -maxdepth 3 -type d | sort
find outputs/section_checkpoints -maxdepth 3 -type f | sort
```

That will show the real layout.

If you want folder-by-folder:
```bash
for d in outputs/section_checkpoints/run_*; do
  echo "==== $d ===="
  find "$d" -maxdepth 3
done
```

### Another important issue
Your `dev` branch is **not clean anymore**, so before doing more pulls/checkouts, protect your work.

If these edits are accidental and you want a clean `dev`, inspect first:
```bash
git diff -- DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r
git diff -- R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R
```

```bash

rm DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.txt
```

If they matter, stash them:
```bash
git stash push -u -m "temp local edits on dev while investigating review outputs"
```

The repo clearly contains:
- `build_company_resolution()` in `R/02_ingest/01_company_resolution.r`

  `data/00_reference/company_map_217.rds`

That strongly suggests the intended flow is:
1. set review mode,
2. populate `review_sample_tickers`,
3. run pipeline,
4. emit outputs under `outputs/section_checkpoints/...`

### Most likely problem now
Not a missing script anymore. More likely one of:
- R ran previously but failed before writing section subfolders/files,
- local edits changed behavior,
- outputs were written somewhere else,
- script was run from a different working directory/environment.

################################################################################
# DAII 3.5 - COMPLETE INTEGRATED PIPELINE - FINAL FIXED VERSION
# Version: 5.3 FINAL | Date: 2026-03-18 (with post‑review corrections)
#
# SECTION INDEX:
#   1  DATA INTEGRATION (live data sources)
#   2  LOAD HOLDINGS DATA (DUMAC portfolio)
#   3  CREATE MASTER TICKER LIST
#   4  LOAD PRE‑FETCHED PANEL DATA
#   5  CREATE COMPANY SNAPSHOT (one row per ticker)
#   6  PROCEED TO MAIN PIPELINE (load snapshot, ensure columns)
#   7  INNOVATION SCORING
#   8  MERGE HOLDINGS DATA (add portfolio flags)
#   9  AI INTENSITY SCORING & PORTFOLIO CONSTRUCTION
#  10  AI CUBE & ANOMALY DETECTION
#  11  AGGRESSIVE AI ANALYSIS (UNICORN HUNTING)
#  12  BENCHMARK CONSTITUENTS & COMPARISON
#  13  BENCHMARK AI EXPOSURE ANALYSIS
#  14  CONTROL BENCHMARKS (AI‑Low, Matched‑Pair)
#  15  PERFORMANCE ATTRIBUTION
#  16  UNIVERSAL CONTROL PANEL (OPTIONAL)
#  17  OUTPUT GENERATION
################################################################################

# Load required packages
library(dplyr)
library(magrittr)
library(DBI)
library(odbc)
library(here)

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Review Initialization
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

source(here("R", "01_utils", "review_support.R"))

review_mode <- is_review_mode()
review_output_dir <- NULL

if (review_mode) {
  review_output_dir <- setup_review_output_dir()
  review_log("Reviewer output directory:", review_output_dir)
}

review_sample_tickers <- NULL
if (review_mode && exists("review_sample_tickers", envir = .GlobalEnv)) {
  review_sample_tickers <- get("review_sample_tickers", envir = .GlobalEnv)
}

# =============================================================================
# SECTION 1: DATA INTEGRATION (LIVE DATA SOURCES)
# =============================================================================
cat(paste(rep("=", 80), collapse = ""), "\n")
cat("📊 SECTION 1: INTEGRATING LIVE DATA SOURCES\n")
cat(paste(rep("=", 80), collapse = ""), "\n\n")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1.1 Source all ingestion scripts
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
source(here("R", "01_utils", "database_connection.r"))
source(here("R", "02_ingest", "01_company_resolution.r"))
source(here("R", "02_ingest", "02_pull_rd_history.r"))
source(here("R", "02_ingest", "03_pull_holdings.r"))
source(here("R", "02_ingest", "04_pull_daily_ratios.r"))
source(here("R", "02_ingest", "05_patentsview.r"))
source(here("R", "02_ingest", "06_pull_bloomberg_api.r"))
source(here("R", "02_ingest", "07_load_bloomberg_cache.r"))
source(here("R", "02_ingest", "09_pull_msci.r"))
source(here("R", "03_transform", "01_create_snapshot.r"))

sganesan@DESKTOP-KNAKJFM MINGW64 ~/DAII-3.5-Framework/DAII-Framework-2026 (dev)
$ sed -n '1,120p' R/02_ingest/01_company_resolution.r
# ============================================================================
# INGEST: Build Company Resolution Table (Ticker → RepNo)
# Version: 7.5 | Date: 2026-03-24
# Description: Maps tickers to Refinitiv RepNos with TRBC industry data
# ============================================================================

source("R/01_utils/database_connection.r")

build_company_resolution <- function(tickers = NULL) {
  message("🔍 Building company resolution table...")

  if(is.null(tickers)) {
    if(file.exists("data/00_reference/company_map_217.rds")) {
      company_map_ref <- readRDS("data/00_reference/company_map_217.rds")
      tickers <- company_map_ref$ticker
      message("   Using full company map with ", length(tickers), " tickers")
    } else {
      stop("No tickers provided and company_map_217.rds not found")
    }
  }

  conn <- connect_research()
  on.exit(dbDisconnect(conn), add = TRUE)

  ticker_list <- paste0("'", tickers, "'", collapse = ", ")

  sql <- sprintf("
    SELECT DISTINCT
        i.Xref_Ticker                       AS Ticker,
        i.RepNo                             AS RepNo,
        c.CompanyName                       AS CompanyName,
        i.Xref_ISIN                         AS ISIN,
        i.ExchangeCode                      AS Exchange,
        i.ExchangeCountry                   AS Country,
        trbc.Description                    AS TRBC_Industry
    FROM refinitiv.class20_refinfo_issue i
    JOIN refinitiv.class20_refinfo_comp c ON c.RepNo = i.RepNo
    LEFT JOIN refinitiv.class20_refinfo_comp_indycls trbc
        ON trbc.RepNo = i.RepNo AND trbc.Type = 'TRBC'
    WHERE i.Xref_Ticker IN (%s)
      AND i.IssueID = 1
      AND c.FilingStatus = 'Filing'
      AND c.LatestInformation_of_InterimFinancials >= '2024-01-01'
    ORDER BY i.Xref_Ticker
  ", ticker_list)

  message("   Querying database for company mappings...")

  company_map <- dbGetQuery(conn, sql)

  message(sprintf("   ✅ Found %d companies with RepNo mapping", nrow(company_map)))

  return(company_map)
}
```

## Copilot

Yes — this chunk reveals the core issue.

## Direct answer
Your local `dev` file **was manually edited** so the review runner now calls:

```r
build_company_resolution(tickers = review_sample_tickers)
```

instead of the original:

```r
build_company_resolution()
```

That explains why `git status` shows `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` as modified, and it strongly suggests you were in the middle of testing or patching review behavior locally.

## What changed
Earlier grep showed the original code path:

```r
company_map <- build_company_resolution()
```

Later grep showed:

```r
if (review_mode && !is.null(review_sample_tickers) && length(review_sample_tickers) > 0) {
  company_map <- build_company_resolution(tickers = review_sample_tickers)
} else {
  company_map <- build_company_resolution()
}
```

So your working copy is no longer the clean remote `dev` version.

## Why this matters
From `R/02_ingest/01_company_resolution.r`:

```r
build_company_resolution <- function(tickers = NULL) {
```

and if `tickers` is NULL, it falls back to:

```r
data/00_reference/company_map_217.rds
```

But if `review_sample_tickers` is passed in, it skips that fallback and builds only for the review sample.

So your local edit is actually a **plausible fix** for review mode, because the original pipeline:
1. built the full company map first,
2. then filtered it afterward.

Your patch changes it to:
1. build only the review sample up front,
2. then continue.

That may have been exactly the intended correction.

## What is still unresolved
`find outputs/section_checkpoints -maxdepth 3 -type f` returns nothing.

That means:
- either no review run completed successfully in this working tree,
- or only empty run directories were created,
- or the run aborted before writing files.

## Important practical conclusion
At this point, your biggest blockers are:

1. **You have uncommitted local code changes on `dev`**
2. **No artifact files exist in `outputs/section_checkpoints/...`**
3. **You previously had an `Rscript` availability problem**
4. You may be testing a local fix that has not been committed yet

## Best next step
Before doing anything else, preserve the current local edits explicitly.

### Option A: stash them
```bash
git stash push -u -m "local review-mode company_map patch and related edits"
```

### Option B: commit them to a temp branch
Safer if you care about this exact state:
```bash
git checkout -b debug/review-sample-company-map
git add DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.txt
git commit -m "WIP: local review mode testing"
```

I recommend **Option B**.

## Also important
You should inspect what changed in the Bloomberg file too:

```bash
git diff -- R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R
```

because that file is also locally modified and may be relevant to why the review run didn't produce artifacts.

## Most likely interpretation of the empty output folders
`setup_review_output_dir()` probably created run directories, but the script likely failed before writing section files. That would fit:
- run folder names exist,
- no files inside,
- review mode initialized,
- pipeline likely stopped early.

## Copilot

Great — this chunk shows **real progress**.

## Direct answer
You successfully got **Section 1 artifacts** written for a run:

- `outputs/section_checkpoints/run_20260602_204133/section1/section1_company_map.csv`
- `outputs/section_checkpoints/run_20260602_204133/section1/section1_company_map_validation.txt`
- `outputs/section_checkpoints/run_20260602_204133/section1/section1_rd_history_sample.csv`
- `outputs/section_checkpoints/run_20260602_204133/section1/section1_rd_history_validation.txt`

So the patched review flow did run far enough to produce Section 1 outputs.

## What that means
Your local change in `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` is not random anymore — it appears to be part of a working review-mode fix.

Also, your grep shows review ticker loading was moved directly into the main pipeline file:

- `review_mode <- is_review_mode()`
- reads `data/00_reference/n25_representative_sample.csv`
- computes `review_sample_tickers`
- errors if the CSV is missing

That means the review driver is no longer the only place providing the sample tickers.

## Important observations from this chunk

### 1. Section 1 works
Artifacts exist under:
- `run_20260602_204133/section1`

So earlier empty runs were likely partial/failed runs, and this run is the first useful one.

### 2. You now have new untracked data outputs
- `data/01_raw/current_holdings.rds`
- `data/01_raw/daily_ratios.rds`

These are generated runtime files, not necessarily files you should commit.

### 3. Snapshot save bug was checked
You verified:
```r
saveRDS(master_snapshot, snapshot_file)
```
So there isn’t a `company_snapshot` vs `master_snapshot` mismatch anymore.

### 4. PatentsView code has defensive checks
`R/02_ingest/05_patentsview.r` now contains:
- company_map column normalization
- assignee column validation
- invalid search pattern warning path

That suggests the later failure may be in patent data generation or reuse, not in Section 1.

### 5. You removed cached patent data
```bash
rm data/01_raw/patent_data.rds
```
That implies you were preparing to force regeneration of patent data on the next run.

## Best interpretation of the current state
At this point, the story looks like this:

- clean upstream `dev` gained review support
- local testing uncovered that review mode still built the full company map too early
- you patched `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` to pass `review_sample_tickers` into `build_company_resolution()`
- that patch enabled Section 1 outputs to be created successfully
- you were then investigating downstream failures, likely around patents/snapshot/next sections

## What I would call the likely meaningful local changes
These are probably intentional, not accidental:
- `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`
- `R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R`

The `.txt` file is likely just a scratch/export copy and probably not meant for commit.

## Recommended next step
You should preserve this state in Git now, because it contains a reproducible improvement.

### Safe route
```bash
git checkout -b debug/review-mode-section1-working
git add DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R
git commit -m "WIP: review mode builds company map from sample tickers"
```

Do **not** add:
- `data/01_raw/current_holdings.rds`
- `data/01_raw/daily_ratios.rds`
- `outputs/section_checkpoints/...`
- `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.txt`

unless you explicitly want runtime artifacts/versioned outputs.

## Very likely next diagnostic target
Since Section 1 files exist but not later sections, the next thing to inspect would be whether the run stopped:
- in Section 2 holdings,
- in Section 3 master ticker list,
- or later in patent processing.

Useful next commands would have been:
```bash
find outputs/section_checkpoints/run_20260602_204133 -maxdepth 3 -type f | sort
grep -n "stop_if_review_section_complete" DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r
```

That would tell us whether the run was intentionally configured to stop after Section 1, 2, or 3.

## Copilot

You ended up in a **rebase conflict after upstream `dev` moved**, and you had already committed your local fixes into two commits.

## Current state
At the end of this chunk:

- local branch: `dev`
- remote branch `origin/dev` advanced from `f1f9d69` to `06b5621`
- your local commits:
  - `78df51e` — `Fix review-mode data ingestion and snapshot dedup for sections 1-3`
  - `61ad4e2` — `Finalize snapshot and Bloomberg review-mode fixes`
- `git pull --rebase origin dev` started rebasing those two commits
- conflict happened in:
  - first `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`
  - later `R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R`
- `R/03_transform/01_create_snapshot.r` was staged during rebase
- untracked runtime/data files were still present but not committed

## Most important interpretation
The conflict snippet you pasted shows that **upstream had already added part of your company_map review-mode fix**.

Specifically, in `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`, both sides appear to contain:

```r
if (review_mode && !is.null(review_sample_tickers) && length(review_sample_tickers) > 0) {
  company_map <- build_company_resolution(tickers = review_sample_tickers)
} else {
  company_map <- build_company_resolution()
}
```

So that conflict was likely just duplicated or overlapping edits, not a true logic disagreement.

## Practical recommendation
If you’re still stuck at that point, the right move is usually:

1. resolve conflict by keeping the correct merged code,
2. `git add` the conflicted file,
3. `git rebase --continue`,
4. resolve any remaining conflict,
5. `git push origin dev`

## The files that matter
### Code commits
Tracked and intended:
- `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`
- `R/02_ingest/05_patentsview.r`
- `R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R`
- `R/03_transform/01_create_snapshot.r`

### Do **not** accidentally commit
These look like generated/runtime artifacts:
- `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.txt`
- `data/01_raw/current_holdings.rds`
- `data/01_raw/daily_ratios.rds`
- `data/01_raw/european_patents.rds`
- `data/01_raw/patent_data.rds`
- `data/01_raw/uspto_bulk/`
- `data/01_raw/wipo_checklist.md`
- `data/01_raw/wipo_quick_reference.txt`
- `data/02_processed/`

## What I infer about the two commits
### Commit 1: `78df51e`
Included only:
- `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r`
- `R/02_ingest/05_patentsview.r`

### Commit 2: `61ad4e2`
Included:
- `R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R`
- `R/03_transform/01_create_snapshot.r`

That split looks clean and intentional.

## If you want the minimal rebase-resolution workflow
Once conflict markers are fixed:

```bash
git add DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r
git rebase --continue
```

Then if Bloomberg file conflicts:
```bash
git add R/02_ingest/06_pull_bloomberg_api.R
git rebase --continue
```

Then verify:
```bash
git status
git log --oneline -5
```

If rebase completes:
```bash
git push origin dev
```

## If things got messy
Safer fallback:
```bash
git rebase --abort
```

That returns you to pre-rebase local state with your two commits still on top of old `dev`.

Then you could create a safety branch:
```bash
git checkout -b backup/review-mode-fixes
```

and retry from there.

## Biggest takeaway from the history so far
You were **not just debugging blindly** — you actually produced and committed substantive fixes for:
- review-mode data ingestion
- patentsview handling
- snapshot logic
- Bloomberg-related review behavior

And the push only failed because remote `dev` changed before you pushed.

1. **Summarize the exact safe Git commands you should run next**, or
2. **Help reconstruct what likely belongs in the conflicted files** from this history.

````
title: "DAII 3.5 Reviewer Orientation – Sprint 1 (Sections 1–3)"
author: "DAII Team"
date: "June 2026"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
numbersections: true
---

\newpage

# Quick Start: Reviewer Run Instructions

## Clone the repository

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
git checkout dev
Open the repo in RStudio
Run the Sprint 1 reviewer wrapper
Do not directly source DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r unless you need to inspect code only.
Use the dedicated wrapper:

bash
Rscript run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R
The wrapper:

Enables review mode (RUN_REVIEW_MODE=true)

Loads the N=25 sample from data/00_reference/n25_representative_sample.csv

Stops after Section 3 (STOP_AFTER_SECTION=3)

Writes checkpoint outputs under outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/

Inspect Section 1 outputs
bash
ls outputs/section_checkpoints/run_*/section1/
Expected artifacts:

section1_company_map.csv + validation

section1_rd_history_sample.csv + validation

section1_master_snapshot.rds / .csv + validation

For this PR (#4), review only Section 1 code and its generated artifacts.

\newpage

Current Review Scope (Sprint 1)
PR	Section	Title
#4	1	Data Integration
#5	2	Load Holdings Data
#6	3	Create Master Ticker List
Approval applies only to the section named in each PR body.
Sections 4–17 are out of scope for Sprint 1 approval.

\newpage

What is DAII?
DAII (Duke AI Investment Intelligence) is a research‑grade quantitative investment framework that systematically identifies companies with high innovation and AI adoption potential.

It combines:

Fundamental financial data

Patent analytics (USPTO + EPO)

Portfolio holdings (DUMAC warehouse)

Market data (Refinitiv, Bloomberg)

Downstream scoring and portfolio construction logic

The framework produces structured outputs for analysis, reporting, and thematic portfolio construction (e.g., AI Leaders, Quality Innovators, Unicorn watchlists).

The project is implemented as an end‑to‑end R pipeline that ingests live data from multiple sources and outputs timestamped CSV files.

\newpage

High‑Level Architecture
The pipeline is organised into 17 numbered sections.

Sprint 1 covers Sections 1–3 (ingestion and preparation layer).
Sections 4–17 are included below only to explain downstream dependencies – they are not in review scope.

Sections 1–3: Ingestion & Preparation
Section	Purpose	Primary Output	Reviewer Focus
1 – Data Integration	Company resolution, R&D history, patents, daily ratios, Bloomberg cache	master_snapshot	Joins, mappings, deduplication, checkpoint outputs
2 – Load Holdings	Match company universe to DUMAC portfolio holdings	holdings_lookup	ISIN/name matching, aggregation, weights, output integrity
3 – Master Ticker List	Build unified ticker universe for downstream use	master_tickers	Completeness, deduplication, casing, checkpoint outputs
Sections 4–17: Downstream Context (Out of Scope)
These sections use the outputs of Sections 1–3 for:

Innovation scoring

AI intensity scoring

Portfolio construction (Quality Innovators, AI Concentrated, Balanced Growth)

Benchmarking (S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, AI‑Low, matched‑pair controls)

Unicorn hunting (aggressive scoring, anomaly detection)

Output generation (timestamped CSV files)

They are not part of the current Sprint 1 approval.

\newpage

Key Data Sources & Their Roles
Source	Data	Used In
Refinitiv (Research DB)	Company fundamentals, R&D, revenue, daily prices, volatility	Section 1 + downstream
DUMAC Warehouse	Current holdings (ISIN, security name, exposure)	Sections 1–2
USPTO Bulk Files	Assignee disambiguation, CPC classification	Section 1 patent integration
EPO API	European patents	Optional Section 1 enrichment
Bloomberg Cache	Monthly prices, 12‑month volatility	Optional Section 1 enrichment
Local reference files	n25_representative_sample.csv, cached RDS files	Sprint 1 review mode
\newpage

Review‑Mode Feature (Sprint 1)
To support technical review without running the full pipeline, Sprint 1 introduces a lightweight observability layer.

Behavior
Activated by RUN_REVIEW_MODE=true

Uses N=25 reviewer sample from data/00_reference/n25_representative_sample.csv

Writes checkpoint outputs under:

outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/section1/

outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/section2/

outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/section3/

Produces CSV/RDS checkpoints + validation summaries (row counts, missingness, duplicates)

Supports early exit via STOP_AFTER_SECTION (e.g., STOP_AFTER_SECTION=1 stops after Section 1)

Intent
These additions increase reviewer visibility into intermediate outputs while leaving normal execution unchanged when review mode is off.

\newpage

What Sprint 1 Reviewers Should Focus On
Section 1 – Data Integration
Correctness of company identifier resolution (ticker → RepNo, ISIN, industry)

Correctness of joins across source systems

Reasonableness of R&D and financial history

Patent classification and aggregation (CPC codes, AI pattern matching)

Missing‑data handling and deduplication (one row per ticker in master_snapshot)

Whether review‑mode checkpoints are produced as documented

Section 2 – Load Holdings Data
Correctness of ISIN‑based matching

Robustness of fallback name‑based matching (cleaning of suffixes like “Inc”, “Corp”)

Avoidance of unintended many‑to‑many duplication

Exposure aggregation and normalised portfolio weights

Clean defaults for unmatched companies (matched_by = "NONE")

Checkpoint outputs

Section 3 – Master Ticker List
Completeness of the union of upstream ticker sources

Correctness of deduplication

Ticker case consistency and downstream casing assumptions

Checkpoint outputs

\newpage

Reproducibility & Execution Assumptions
Reviewers should be aware of the following prerequisites:

Requirement	Mandatory?	Notes
Refinitiv Research DB access	Yes	For live data pulls
DUMAC Warehouse access	Yes	For holdings retrieval
USPTO bulk files	Yes (or auto‑refresh)	Falls back to download if missing/stale (>90 days)
Bloomberg cache	No	Pipeline continues without it
n25_representative_sample.csv	Yes (review mode)	Must exist at data/00_reference/
No hidden interactive setup	–	Wrapper should run cleanly from Rscript
The intended reviewer path is via the wrapper – not by manually sourcing the main script.

\newpage

Common Reviewer Friction Points
Document any of these findings in PR comments:

Missing or undocumented local prerequisites

Hidden assumptions about object names or prior section outputs

Ticker casing inconsistencies across intermediate objects

Optional external source unavailability (Bloomberg, patent data)

Mismatch between documented checkpoint outputs and actual outputs

\newpage

Suggested Reviewer Sequence
Read this orientation document.

Open the relevant PR (#4, #5, or #6).

Read the PR body and the reviewer quick‑start comment.

Open the primary files listed in the PR.

Execute the Sprint 1 review wrapper (if local execution is part of your review).

Inspect the corresponding section checkpoint outputs.

Leave review comments in the PR (code‑level, decomposition, execution observations).

Use Jira for severity assignment and governance disposition.

\newpage

Final Note
This orientation document provides a concise mental model of DAII and Sprint 1 scope.
It does not replace the PR‑specific scope and instructions. For approval decisions, the source of truth is:

The PR body

Follow‑up PR comments

The actual in‑scope code regions
````
What are your thoughts on the text below for the Sprint 1/Section 1 orientation document?



## Copilot

Strong draft overall. My main view: **the intent is good, but the document needs cleanup before sharing because the Quick Start section currently mixes prose and shell commands in a way that will confuse reviewers, and a few statements no longer match the repo history you pasted.**

## What’s working
- **Good reviewer framing.** It clearly narrows Sprint 1 into PRs `#4`, `#5`, `#6`.
- **Good separation of scope vs context.** Sections 4–17 are explained without expanding review scope.
- **Good explanation of review mode.** The checkpoint/output idea is easy to understand.
- **Good reviewer focus areas.** The Section 1 checklist is useful and concrete.
- **Good note about wrapper-first execution.** That’s an important operational safeguard.

## Biggest issues to fix

### 1. The Quick Start block is malformed
Right now this part is confusing:

```bash
git clone ...
cd ...
git checkout dev
Open the repo in RStudio
Run the Sprint 1 reviewer wrapper
Do not directly source ...
Use the dedicated wrapper:
```

That mixes terminal commands with narrative text inside one code fence.

Also this appears as plain text:

```text
bash
Rscript run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R
```

That should be a real fenced code block.

**Recommendation:** split prose from commands.

### 2. The expected Section 1 outputs may be incomplete or inconsistent with observed behavior
From the history you shared, Section 1 outputs definitely included:
- `section1_company_map.csv`
- `section1_company_map_validation.txt`
- `section1_rd_history_sample.csv`
- `section1_rd_history_validation.txt`

And the code also references:
- `section1_master_snapshot.rds`
- `section1_master_snapshot.csv`
- `section1_master_snapshot_validation.txt`

So the document should either:
- say these are the **expected** artifacts when the run completes through the end of Section 1, or
- clarify that partial runs may produce only a subset.

### 3. The wrapper instructions assume `Rscript` is available
Your own chat history showed:
```bash
bash: Rscript: command not found
```

So for reviewer onboarding, add a one-line prerequisite like:

- “Ensure `Rscript` is available on your PATH before running the wrapper.”

Otherwise reviewers may think the repo is broken when it’s just an environment issue.

### 4. “Open the repo in RStudio” conflicts slightly with command-line wrapper guidance
Not a major issue, but it mixes two modes:
- shell execution via `Rscript`
- interactive inspection via RStudio

That’s fine if intentional, but I’d phrase it as optional:

- “Optionally open the repo in RStudio for code inspection.”
- “Use the wrapper via `Rscript` for execution.”

### 5. Some claims should be made slightly more precise
Examples:
- “Wrapper should run cleanly from Rscript” is aspirational; better as “The intended reviewer path is to run the wrapper via `Rscript`.”
- “No hidden interactive setup” is good, but you may want “no manual sourcing of intermediate objects should be required.”

## Content suggestions for Section 1 specifically
Since this is for Sprint 1 / Section 1 orientation, I’d strengthen the Section 1 guidance with a few explicit checks:

- confirm `company_map` is limited correctly in review mode
- verify one row per ticker in `master_snapshot`
- confirm validation files match actual row counts
- note that optional enrichments like Bloomberg may degrade gracefully rather than hard-fail

That would align better with the behavior you were debugging.

## Suggested edited Quick Start
Here’s a cleaner version of the opening section:

````markdown name=orientation-quick-start.md
# Quick Start: Reviewer Run Instructions

## Clone the repository

```bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
git checkout dev
```

## Open the project

Optionally open the repository in RStudio for code inspection.

## Run the Sprint 1 reviewer wrapper

Do **not** directly source `DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r` unless you only need to inspect code.
Use the dedicated wrapper:

```bash
Rscript run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R
```

Before running the wrapper, ensure `Rscript` is installed and available on your system `PATH`.

The wrapper:

- enables review mode (`RUN_REVIEW_MODE=true`)
- loads the N=25 reviewer sample from `data/00_reference/n25_representative_sample.csv`
- stops after Section 3 (`STOP_AFTER_SECTION=3`)
- writes checkpoint outputs under `outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/`

## Inspect Section 1 outputs

```bash
ls outputs/section_checkpoints/run_*/section1/
```

Expected Section 1 artifacts after a successful Section 1 review-mode run include:

- `section1_company_map.csv`
- `section1_company_map_validation.txt`
- `section1_rd_history_sample.csv`
- `section1_rd_history_validation.txt`
- `section1_master_snapshot.rds`
- `section1_master_snapshot.csv`
- `section1_master_snapshot_validation.txt`

For PR #4, review only Section 1 code and its generated artifacts.
````

## Other small editorial improvements
- Standardize capitalization:
  - “review mode” vs “Review-Mode Feature”
  - “Section 1” vs “section1” for paths
- Use monospace for filenames and env vars consistently:
  - `RUN_REVIEW_MODE`
  - `STOP_AFTER_SECTION`
  - `n25_representative_sample.csv`
- Consider converting the “Current Review Scope” table into proper Markdown table syntax if this is staying in Markdown before Pandoc rendering.
- “timestamped CSV files” may be too narrow since some outputs are `.rds`; maybe say “timestamped checkpoint and output files.”

## Bottom line
**My opinion:** the structure and substance are good, but I would revise it before sending to reviewers. The highest-priority fixes are:
1. clean up the malformed Quick Start formatting,
2. clarify prerequisites around `Rscript`,
3. make expected Section 1 artifacts explicit and accurate,
4. tighten wording so it reflects intended reviewer workflow without overstating reliability.

If you want, I can **rewrite the whole orientation document into a polished reviewer-ready version** while preserving your content and structure.

## @sganesan_dumac

How do I use 2?

## Copilot

Here are both.

## 1. PR-ready summary blurb

You can paste this into a PR description or comment:

```markdown
## Reviewer Orientation Document

For Sprint 1 review context, see:

`review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.md`

This document provides:
- reviewer quick-start instructions
- Sprint 1 scope boundaries for PRs #4, #5, and #6
- high-level DAII architecture context
- review-mode behavior and expected checkpoint outputs
- section-specific reviewer focus areas
- reproducibility assumptions and common friction points

For approval decisions, the source of truth remains:
- the PR body
- follow-up PR comments
- the actual in-scope code regions
```

## 2. Pandoc-friendly polished pass

What I mean by this:
- the file is already Markdown and reasonably Pandoc-compatible
- a “Pandoc-friendly polished pass” means tightening it specifically for export to PDF/Word/HTML using Pandoc
- this usually involves:
  - cleaner spacing and headings
  - proper tables
  - predictable page breaks
  - code fences that render well
  - optional YAML metadata improvements
  - avoiding formatting that looks fine in GitHub but exports poorly

## How to use 2

If your goal is **to generate a reviewer PDF**, the usual flow is:

### Step A: keep the Markdown file in the repo
You already have:
- `review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.md`

### Step B: install Pandoc
Check if Pandoc exists:

```bash
pandoc --version
```

If not installed:
- install Pandoc on Windows
- then reopen your shell

### Step C: generate a PDF
From the repo root:

```bash
pandoc review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.md -o review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.pdf
```

### Step D: if PDF generation fails
Pandoc PDF export often needs a PDF engine such as LaTeX.

Try:

```bash
pandoc review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.md -o review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.pdf --pdf-engine=xelatex
```

If `xelatex` is missing, install a LaTeX distribution such as:
- TinyTeX
- MiKTeX
- TeX Live

### Step E: HTML or Word are often easier
If PDF tooling is annoying, export HTML or DOCX instead:

```bash
pandoc review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.md -o review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.html
```

```bash
pandoc review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.md -o review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3.docx
```

## Practical recommendation
For reviewers, the easiest distribution options are usually:

- **GitHub Markdown in the repo** for source of truth
- **PDF export** for a polished handout
- **PR comment linking to the file** for visibility

## Suggested workflow
1. Keep the Markdown file in `review/sprint-1/`
2. Paste the PR blurb into the relevant PRs
3. Export a PDF if you want a presentation-quality reviewer packet
4. Share either:
   - the repo file link
   - the generated PDF
   - or both

## If you want the polished pass from me
I can also produce a **second version of the file optimized specifically for Pandoc/PDF export**, for example:
- cleaner title page
- optional version/date block
- better page break handling
- slightly more formal section formatting
- print-friendly wording

If you want, I can create a companion file like:

`review/sprint-1/reviewer-orientation-sections-1-3-pandoc.md`

and make it specifically tuned for export.

DAII 3.5 Reviewer Orientation – Sprint 1 (Sections 1–3)
i Everybody! – This document orients reviewers for the Sprint 1 technical review of DAII 3.5.
It covers only Sections 1–3 (Data Integration, Load Holdings, Master Ticker List). Sections 4–17 are mentioned only for downstream context and are not in scope for approval.

1. Quick Start: Reviewer Run Instructions
1.1 Clone the repository
bash
git clone https://github.com/dumac-duke-edu/DAII-Framework-2026.git
cd DAII-Framework-2026
git checkout dev
1.2 Open the repo in RStudio
1.3 Run the Sprint 1 reviewer wrapper
Do not directly source DAII_3.5_TRUE_For_Review_06_01_2026.r unless you need to inspect code only.
Use the dedicated wrapper:

bash
Rscript run_review_sprint1_sections_1_3.R
The wrapper:

Enables review mode (RUN_REVIEW_MODE=true)

Loads the N=25 sample from data/00_reference/n25_representative_sample.csv

Stops after Section 3 (STOP_AFTER_SECTION=3)

Writes checkpoint outputs under outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/

1.4 Inspect Section 1 outputs
bash
ls outputs/section_checkpoints/run_*/section1/
Expected artifacts:

section1_company_map.csv + validation

section1_rd_history_sample.csv + validation

section1_master_snapshot.rds / .csv + validation

For this PR (#4), review only Section 1 code and its generated artifacts.

2. Current Review Scope (Sprint 1)
PR	Section	Title
#4	1	Data Integration
#5	2	Load Holdings Data
#6	3	Create Master Ticker List
Approval applies only to the section named in each PR body.
Sections 4–17 are out of scope for Sprint 1 approval.

3. What is DAII?
DAII (Duke AI Investment Intelligence) is a research‑grade quantitative investment framework that systematically identifies companies with high innovation and AI adoption potential.

It combines:

Fundamental financial data

Patent analytics (USPTO + EPO)

Portfolio holdings (DUMAC warehouse)

Market data (Refinitiv, Bloomberg)

Downstream scoring and portfolio construction logic

The framework produces structured outputs for analysis, reporting, and thematic portfolio construction (e.g., AI Leaders, Quality Innovators, Unicorn watchlists).

The project is implemented as an end‑to‑end R pipeline that ingests live data from multiple sources and outputs timestamped CSV files.

4. High‑Level Architecture
The pipeline is organised into 17 numbered sections.

Sprint 1 covers Sections 1–3 (ingestion and preparation layer).
Sections 4–17 are included below only to explain downstream dependencies – they are not in review scope.

Sections 1–3: Ingestion & Preparation
Section	Purpose	Primary Output	Reviewer Focus
1 – Data Integration	Company resolution, R&D history, patents, daily ratios, Bloomberg cache	master_snapshot	Joins, mappings, deduplication, checkpoint outputs
2 – Load Holdings	Match company universe to DUMAC portfolio holdings	holdings_lookup	ISIN/name matching, aggregation, weights, output integrity
3 – Master Ticker List	Build unified ticker universe for downstream use	master_tickers	Completeness, deduplication, casing, checkpoint outputs
Sections 4–17: Downstream Context (Out of Scope)
These sections use the outputs of Sections 1–3 for:

Innovation scoring

AI intensity scoring

Portfolio construction (Quality Innovators, AI Concentrated, Balanced Growth)

Benchmarking (S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, AI‑Low, matched‑pair controls)

Unicorn hunting (aggressive scoring, anomaly detection)

Output generation (timestamped CSV files)

They are not part of the current Sprint 1 approval.

5. Key Data Sources & Their Roles
Source	Data	Used In
Refinitiv (Research DB)	Company fundamentals, R&D, revenue, daily prices, volatility	Section 1 + downstream
DUMAC Warehouse	Current holdings (ISIN, security name, exposure)	Sections 1–2
USPTO Bulk Files	Assignee disambiguation, CPC classification	Section 1 patent integration
EPO API	European patents	Optional Section 1 enrichment
Bloomberg Cache	Monthly prices, 12‑month volatility	Optional Section 1 enrichment
Local reference files	n25_representative_sample.csv, cached RDS files	Sprint 1 review mode
6. Review‑Mode Feature (Sprint 1)
To support technical review without running the full pipeline, Sprint 1 introduces a lightweight observability layer.

Behavior
Activated by RUN_REVIEW_MODE=true

Uses N=25 reviewer sample from data/00_reference/n25_representative_sample.csv

Writes checkpoint outputs under:

outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/section1/

outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/section2/

outputs/section_checkpoints/run_<timestamp>/section3/

Produces CSV/RDS checkpoints + validation summaries (row counts, missingness, duplicates)

Supports early exit via STOP_AFTER_SECTION (e.g., STOP_AFTER_SECTION=1 stops after Section 1)

Intent
These additions increase reviewer visibility into intermediate outputs while leaving normal execution unchanged when review mode is off.

7. What Sprint 1 Reviewers Should Focus On
Section 1 – Data Integration
Correctness of company identifier resolution (ticker → RepNo, ISIN, industry)

Correctness of joins across source systems

Reasonableness of R&D and financial history

Patent classification and aggregation (CPC codes, AI pattern matching)

Missing‑data handling and deduplication (one row per ticker in master_snapshot)

Whether review‑mode checkpoints are produced as documented

Section 2 – Load Holdings Data
Correctness of ISIN‑based matching

Robustness of fallback name‑based matching (cleaning of suffixes like “Inc”, “Corp”)

Avoidance of unintended many‑to‑many duplication

Exposure aggregation and normalised portfolio weights

Clean defaults for unmatched companies (matched_by = "NONE")

Checkpoint outputs

Section 3 – Master Ticker List
Completeness of the union of upstream ticker sources

Correctness of deduplication

Ticker case consistency and downstream casing assumptions

Checkpoint outputs

8. Reproducibility & Execution Assumptions
Reviewers should be aware of the following prerequisites:

Requirement	Mandatory?	Notes
Refinitiv Research DB access	Yes	For live data pulls
DUMAC Warehouse access	Yes	For holdings retrieval
USPTO bulk files	Yes (or auto‑refresh)	Falls back to download if missing/stale (>90 days)
Bloomberg cache	No	Pipeline continues without it
n25_representative_sample.csv	Yes (review mode)	Must exist at data/00_reference/
No hidden interactive setup	–	Wrapper should run cleanly from Rscript
The intended reviewer path is via the wrapper – not by manually sourcing the main script.

9. Common Reviewer Friction Points
Document any of these findings in PR comments:

Missing or undocumented local prerequisites

Hidden assumptions about object names or prior section outputs

Ticker casing inconsistencies across intermediate objects

Optional external source unavailability (Bloomberg, patent data)

Mismatch between documented checkpoint outputs and actual outputs

10. Suggested Reviewer Sequence
Read this orientation document.

Open the relevant PR (#4, #5, or #6).

Read the PR body and the reviewer quick‑start comment.

Open the primary files listed in the PR.

Execute the Sprint 1 review wrapper (if local execution is part of your review).

Inspect the corresponding section checkpoint outputs.

Leave review comments in the PR (code‑level, decomposition, execution observations).

Use Jira for severity assignment and governance disposition.

11. Final Note
This orientation document provides a concise mental model of DAII and Sprint 1 scope.
It does not replace the PR‑specific scope and instructions. For approval decisions, the source of truth is:

The PR body

Follow‑up PR comments

The actual in‑scope code regions

Prepared for Sprint 1 Code Review – DAII 3.5, June 2026

## Copilot
